hypothesis: We hypothesize that for supported metal nanoparticles where the solid and liquid contact angles diverge significantly (e.g., by engineering substrate surface energy or chemistry), the melting temperature will deviate measurably and predictably from that of a free spherical particle of equivalent curvature, with the magnitude of depression or elevation scaling systematically with the difference in solid-liquid contact angles as described by the thermodynamic model.
raw_output: We hypothesize that for supported metal nanoparticles where the solid and liquid contact angles diverge significantly (e.g., by engineering substrate surface energy or chemistry), the melting temperature will deviate measurably and predictably from that of a free spherical particle of equivalent curvature, with the magnitude of depression or elevation scaling systematically with the difference in solid-liquid contact angles as described by the thermodynamic model.

abstract: We study finite-temperature phase transitions in a two-dimensional boson Hubbard model with zero-point quantum fluctuations via Monte Carlo simulations of quantum rotor model, and construct the corresponding phase diagram. Compressibility shows a thermally activated gapped behavior in the insulating regime. Finite-size scaling of the superfluid stiffness clearly shows the nature of the Kosterlitz-Thouless transition. The transition temperature, $T_c$, confirms a scaling relation $T_c \propto \rho_0^x$ with $x=1.0$. Some evidences of anomalous quantum behavior at low temperatures are presented.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the anomalous quantum behavior observed at low temperatures in the two-dimensional boson Hubbard model arises from enhanced quantum fluctuations near the Mott insulator-superfluid boundary, which will manifest as a measurable deviation from the linear scaling relation T_c ∝ ρ₀ with an effective exponent x < 1 when the system approaches the quantum critical point, distinguishable from thermal fluctuation effects through finite-size scaling analysis of the superfluid stiffness at temperatures below the Kosterlitz-Thouless transition.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the anomalous quantum behavior observed at low temperatures in the two-dimensional boson Hubbard model arises from enhanced quantum fluctuations near the Mott insulator-superfluid boundary, which will manifest as a measurable deviation from the linear scaling relation T_c ∝ ρ₀ with an effective exponent x < 1 when the system approaches the quantum critical point, distinguishable from thermal fluctuation effects through finite-size scaling analysis of the superfluid stiffness at temperatures below the Kosterlitz-Thouless transition.

abstract: We review the algebraic construction of the S-matrix of AdS/CFT. We also present its symmetry algebra which turns out to be a Yangian of the centrally extended su(2|2) superalgebra.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Yangian symmetry of the centrally extended su(2|2) S-matrix in AdS/CFT can be deformed by a quantum group parameter q related to the string tension, such that in a specific limit this q-deformed Yangian structure reproduces the exact S-matrix of strings on the mirror (q-deformed) AdS₅ × S⁵ background, providing a unified algebraic framework that interpolates between the weak- and strong-coupling regimes of the theory.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the Yangian symmetry of the centrally extended su(2|2) S-matrix in AdS/CFT can be deformed by a quantum group parameter q related to the string tension, such that in a specific limit this q-deformed Yangian structure reproduces the exact S-matrix of strings on the mirror (q-deformed) AdS₅ × S⁵ background, providing a unified algebraic framework that interpolates between the weak- and strong-coupling regimes of the theory.

abstract: We apply well established finite temperature Quantum Monte Carlo techniques to one dimensional Bose systems with soft and hardcore constraint, as well as to spinless fermion systems. We give clear and robust numerical evidence that, as expected, no superfluid density for Bosons or Meissner fraction for fermions. is possible at {\em any} non zero temperature in one dimensional interacting Bose or fermi lattice models, whereas a finite Drude weight is generally observed in gapless systems, in partial disagreement to previous expectations.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that in quasi-one-dimensional ladder geometries (two coupled chains), a finite superfluid density for bosons or Meissner fraction for fermions will emerge at low but nonzero temperatures above a critical interchain coupling strength, providing a sharp crossover boundary in the coupling-temperature phase diagram that can be quantitatively mapped using the same finite-temperature Quantum Monte Carlo techniques applied to the strictly one-dimensional case.
raw_output: We hypothesize that in quasi-one-dimensional ladder geometries (two coupled chains), a finite superfluid density for bosons or Meissner fraction for fermions will emerge at low but nonzero temperatures above a critical interchain coupling strength, providing a sharp crossover boundary in the coupling-temperature phase diagram that can be quantitatively mapped using the same finite-temperature Quantum Monte Carlo techniques applied to the strictly one-dimensional case.

abstract: We study the dc conductance of organic single-molecule contacts in the presence of external electromagnetic radiation (photoconductance). In agreement with previous predictions, we find that the radiation can lead to large enhancements of the conductance of such contacts by bringing off-resonant levels into resonance through photoassisted processes. In our analysis we make use of the simplifying fact that, under certain assumptions, the photoconductance can be expressed in terms of the transmission function in the absence of the radiation. The conductance enhancement is demonstrated for oligophenylene molecules between gold electrodes, whose electronic structure is calculated based on density-functional theory. It is shown that the exponential decay of the conductance with the length of the molecule can be replaced by a length-independent value in the presence of radiation.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the frequency-dependent photoconductance enhancement in oligophenylene single-molecule junctions will exhibit distinct resonance peaks corresponding to transitions between specific molecular orbitals, such that selective tuning of the electromagnetic radiation frequency can achieve orbital-specific conductance control, enabling discrimination between HOMO- and LUMO-mediated transport channels that would be indistinguishable under dc conditions alone.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the frequency-dependent photoconductance enhancement in oligophenylene single-molecule junctions will exhibit distinct resonance peaks corresponding to transitions between specific molecular orbitals, such that selective tuning of the electromagnetic radiation frequency can achieve orbital-specific conductance control, enabling discrimination between HOMO- and LUMO-mediated transport channels that would be indistinguishable under dc conditions alone.

abstract: We present an analytic example of two dimensional quantum mechanical system, where the exponential suppression of the probability of over-barrier reflection changes non-monotonically with energy. The suppression is minimal at certain "optimal" energies where reflection occurs with exponentially larger probability than at other energies.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the non-monotonic energy dependence of over-barrier reflection suppression arises from quantum interference between classically forbidden trajectories in the complex plane, such that the "optimal" energies correspond precisely to stationary-phase conditions where multiple complex saddle-point contributions constructively interfere, a prediction that can be tested by mapping the analytic structure of the transfer matrix in the complex energy plane and verifying that optimal reflection energies coincide with near-degeneracies of dominant saddle points.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the non-monotonic energy dependence of over-barrier reflection suppression arises from quantum interference between classically forbidden trajectories in the complex plane, such that the "optimal" energies correspond precisely to stationary-phase conditions where multiple complex saddle-point contributions constructively interfere, a prediction that can be tested by mapping the analytic structure of the transfer matrix in the complex energy plane and verifying that optimal reflection energies coincide with near-degeneracies of dominant saddle points.

abstract: We report studies of cyclotron resonance in monolayer graphene. Cyclotron resonance is detected using the photoconductive response of the sample for several different Landau level occupancies. The experiments measure an electron velocity at the K- (Dirac) point of $c_{K}^{*}$ = 1.093 x 10$^{6}$ ms$^{-1}$ and in addition detect a significant asymmetry between the electron and hole bands, leading to a difference in the electron and hole velocities of 5% by energies of 125 meV away from the Dirac point.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the observed 5% electron-hole velocity asymmetry in monolayer graphene will exhibit a systematic dependence on substrate dielectric constant, such that placing graphene on high-κ dielectric substrates will suppress this particle-hole asymmetry by screening the electron-electron interactions responsible for renormalizing the band velocities differentially away from the Dirac point, thereby bringing the system closer to ideal linear Dirac cone symmetry.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the observed 5% electron-hole velocity asymmetry in monolayer graphene will exhibit a systematic dependence on substrate dielectric constant, such that placing graphene on high-κ dielectric substrates will suppress this particle-hole asymmetry by screening the electron-electron interactions responsible for renormalizing the band velocities differentially away from the Dirac point, thereby bringing the system closer to ideal linear Dirac cone symmetry.

abstract: Graphene nano-ribbons junctions based electronic devices are proposed in this Letter. Non-equilibrium Green function calculations show that nano-ribbon junctions tailored from single layer graphene with different edge shape and width can act as metal-semiconductor junctions and quantum dots can be implemented. In virtue of the possibilities of patterning monolayer graphene down to atomic precision, these structures, quite different from the previously reported two-dimensional bulk graphene or carbon nanotube devices, are expected to be used as the building blocks of the future nano-electronics.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that graphene nano-ribbon junctions with asymmetric edge geometries (combining armchair and zigzag terminations) at the interface between metallic and semiconducting segments will exhibit rectification behavior with a bias-dependent current ratio that can be systematically tuned by varying the relative widths of the two ribbon segments, enabling the design of atomic-precision graphene-based diodes with predictable current-voltage characteristics.
raw_output: We hypothesize that graphene nano-ribbon junctions with asymmetric edge geometries (combining armchair and zigzag terminations) at the interface between metallic and semiconducting segments will exhibit rectification behavior with a bias-dependent current ratio that can be systematically tuned by varying the relative widths of the two ribbon segments, enabling the design of atomic-precision graphene-based diodes with predictable current-voltage characteristics.

abstract: Using complex plane analysis we show that left-handed slab may support either leaky slab waves, which are backward because of negative refraction, or leaky surface waves, which are backward or forward depending on the propagation direction of the surface wave itself. Moreover, there is a general connection between the reflection coefficient of the left-handed slab and the one of the corresponding right-handed slab (with opposite permittivity and permeability) so that leaky slab modes are excited for the same angle of incidence of the impinging beam for both structures. Many negative giant lateral shifts can be explained by the excitation of these leaky modes.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the angular dependence of giant lateral shifts in left-handed slabs exhibits a discrete resonance spectrum that maps bijectively onto the leaky mode dispersion curves of the corresponding right-handed slab, such that engineering the permittivity-permeability ratio of the right-handed counterpart provides a predictive design rule for selectively enhancing or suppressing specific negative lateral shift resonances in the left-handed structure.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the angular dependence of giant lateral shifts in left-handed slabs exhibits a discrete resonance spectrum that maps bijectively onto the leaky mode dispersion curves of the corresponding right-handed slab, such that engineering the permittivity-permeability ratio of the right-handed counterpart provides a predictive design rule for selectively enhancing or suppressing specific negative lateral shift resonances in the left-handed structure.

abstract: Theoretical description of the field emission of electrons from nanoscale objects weakly coupled to the cathode is presented. It is shown that the field- emission current increases in a step-like fashion due to single-electron charging which leads to abrupt changes of the effective electric field responsible for the field emission. A detailed consideration of the current-voltage characteristics is carried out for a nanocluster modelled by a metallic spherical particle in the close vicinity of the cathode and for a cylindrical silicon nanowire grown on the cathode surface.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the step-like field emission characteristics of weakly coupled nanoclusters will exhibit temperature-dependent suppression of discrete charging steps, transitioning to a smooth continuous current-voltage curve above a critical temperature determined by the ratio of the single-electron charging energy to thermal energy (e²/2C ~ k_BT), thereby providing a direct experimental means to extract the effective nanocluster capacitance from the temperature at which Coulomb staircase features are thermally washed out.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the step-like field emission characteristics of weakly coupled nanoclusters will exhibit temperature-dependent suppression of discrete charging steps, transitioning to a smooth continuous current-voltage curve above a critical temperature determined by the ratio of the single-electron charging energy to thermal energy (e²/2C ~ k_BT), thereby providing a direct experimental means to extract the effective nanocluster capacitance from the temperature at which Coulomb staircase features are thermally washed out.

abstract: The entanglement entropy of the two-dimensional random transverse Ising model is studied with a numerical implementation of the strong disorder renormalization group. The asymptotic behavior of the entropy per surface area diverges at, and only at, the quantum phase transition that is governed by an infinite randomness fixed point. Here we identify a double-logarithmic multiplicative correction to the area law for the entanglement entropy. This contrasts with the pure area law valid at the infinite randomness fixed point in the diluted transverse Ising model in higher dimensions.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the double-logarithmic multiplicative correction to the area law observed at the infinite randomness fixed point of the two-dimensional random transverse Ising model is a universal feature specific to two-dimensional systems with strong disorder, such that analogous two-dimensional random quantum systems in different universality classes (e.g., random Heisenberg or Potts models) will exhibit the same functional form of correction S ~ L log(log L) at their respective infinite randomness fixed points, while three-dimensional counterparts of these same models revert to a pure area law.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the double-logarithmic multiplicative correction to the area law observed at the infinite randomness fixed point of the two-dimensional random transverse Ising model is a universal feature specific to two-dimensional systems with strong disorder, such that analogous two-dimensional random quantum systems in different universality classes (e.g., random Heisenberg or Potts models) will exhibit the same functional form of correction S ~ L log(log L) at their respective infinite randomness fixed points, while three-dimensional counterparts of these same models revert to a pure area law.

abstract: We have performed longitudinal ultrasound (9.5 MHz) attenuation measurements in the B-phase of superfluid $^3$He in 98% porosity aerogel down to the zero temperature limit for a wide range of pressures at zero magnetic field. The absolute attenuation was determined by direct transmission of sound pulses. Compared to the bulk fluid, our results revealed a drastically different behavior in attenuation, which is consistent with theoretical accounts with gapless excitations and a collision drag effect.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying a controlled magnetic field to superfluid ³He in 98% porosity aerogel will systematically suppress the gapless excitations responsible for the anomalous attenuation behavior, producing a measurable field-dependent crossover in longitudinal ultrasound attenuation toward bulk-like behavior at field strengths sufficient to open a full quasiparticle gap, thereby allowing quantitative determination of the energy scale associated with aerogel-induced pair-breaking.
raw_output: We hypothesize that applying a controlled magnetic field to superfluid ³He in 98% porosity aerogel will systematically suppress the gapless excitations responsible for the anomalous attenuation behavior, producing a measurable field-dependent crossover in longitudinal ultrasound attenuation toward bulk-like behavior at field strengths sufficient to open a full quasiparticle gap, thereby allowing quantitative determination of the energy scale associated with aerogel-induced pair-breaking.

abstract: We experimentally demonstrate controlled polarization-selective phenomena in a whispering gallery mode resonator. We observed efficient ($\approx 75 %$) polarization conversion of light in a silica microsphere coupled to a tapered optical fiber with proper optimization of the polarization of the propagating light. A simple model treating the microsphere as a ring resonator provides a good fit to the observed behavior.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that by introducing deliberate geometric asymmetry (ellipticity) into the silica microsphere resonator, the polarization conversion efficiency can be increased beyond 75% by lifting the degeneracy between transverse-electric and transverse-magnetic whispering gallery modes, thereby enabling near-unity polarization conversion at precisely tunable resonance splitting frequencies determined by the degree of ellipticity.
raw_output: We hypothesize that by introducing deliberate geometric asymmetry (ellipticity) into the silica microsphere resonator, the polarization conversion efficiency can be increased beyond 75% by lifting the degeneracy between transverse-electric and transverse-magnetic whispering gallery modes, thereby enabling near-unity polarization conversion at precisely tunable resonance splitting frequencies determined by the degree of ellipticity.

abstract: We study the critical behavior of the three-dimensional $\pm J$ Ising model [with a random-exchange probability $P(J_{xy}) = p \delta(J_{xy} - J) + (1-p) \delta(J_{xy} + J)$] at the transition line between the paramagnetic and ferromagnetic phase, which extends from $p=1$ to a multicritical (Nishimori) point at $p=p_N\approx 0.767$. By a finite-size scaling analysis of Monte Carlo simulations at various values of $p$ in the region $p_N<p<1$, we provide strong numerical evidence that the critical behavior along the ferromagnetic transition line belongs to the same universality class as the three-dimensional randomly-dilute Ising model. We obtain the results $\nu=0.682(3)$ and $\eta=0.036(2)$ for the critical exponents, which are consistent with the estimates $\nu=0.683(2)$ and $\eta=0.036(1)$ at the transition of randomly-dilute Ising models.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the universality class equivalence between the ±J Ising model's ferromagnetic transition line and the randomly-dilute Ising model breaks down at the multicritical Nishimori point (p ≈ 0.767), where the confluence of frustration and disorder produces distinct critical exponents that define a separate, higher-dimensional universality class characterized by measurably different values of ν and η than those observed along the ferromagnetic transition line.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the universality class equivalence between the ±J Ising model's ferromagnetic transition line and the randomly-dilute Ising model breaks down at the multicritical Nishimori point (p ≈ 0.767), where the confluence of frustration and disorder produces distinct critical exponents that define a separate, higher-dimensional universality class characterized by measurably different values of ν and η than those observed along the ferromagnetic transition line.

abstract: Is it possible to understand cancer? Or more specifically, is it possible to understand cancer from genetic side? There already many answers in literature. The most optimistic one has claimed that it is mission-possible. Duesberg and his colleagues reviewed the impressive amount of research results on cancer accumulated over 100 years. It confirms the a general opinion that considering all available experimental results and clinical observations there is no cancer theory without major difficulties, including the prevailing gene-based cancer theories. They have then listed 9 "absolute discrepancies" for such cancer theory. In this letter the quantitative evidence against one of their major reasons for dismissing mutation cancer theory, by both in vivo experiment and a first principle computation, is explicitly pointed out.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the accumulation rate of somatic mutations in cancer cells follows a non-linear, threshold-dependent kinetic model such that below a critical mutation burden, stochastic mutational events contribute negligibly to malignant transformation, whereas above this threshold, cooperative mutational interactions produce exponential increases in oncogenic potential that can be quantitatively predicted from first-principles thermodynamic calculations of genomic instability.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the accumulation rate of somatic mutations in cancer cells follows a non-linear, threshold-dependent kinetic model such that below a critical mutation burden, stochastic mutational events contribute negligibly to malignant transformation, whereas above this threshold, cooperative mutational interactions produce exponential increases in oncogenic potential that can be quantitatively predicted from first-principles thermodynamic calculations of genomic instability.

abstract: We report a new effect of surfactants in pinning a drop contact line, specifically that lysozyme promotes while lauryl sulfate inhibits pinning. We explain the pinning disparity assuming difference in wetting: the protein-laden drop wets a "clean" surface and the surfactant-laden drop wets an auto-precursored surface.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the differential contact line pinning behavior observed between lysozyme and lauryl sulfate can be abolished by pre-coating the substrate with a lauryl sulfate monolayer prior to droplet deposition, such that lysozyme-laden drops on pre-precursored surfaces will exhibit reduced pinning forces quantitatively equivalent to those measured for lauryl sulfate-laden drops on uncoated surfaces.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the differential contact line pinning behavior observed between lysozyme and lauryl sulfate can be abolished by pre-coating the substrate with a lauryl sulfate monolayer prior to droplet deposition, such that lysozyme-laden drops on pre-precursored surfaces will exhibit reduced pinning forces quantitatively equivalent to those measured for lauryl sulfate-laden drops on uncoated surfaces.

abstract: How to effectively solve the eigen solutions of the nonlinear spinor field equation coupling with some other interaction fields is important to understand the behavior of the elementary particles. In this paper, we derive a simplified form of the eigen equation of the nonlinear spinor, and then propose a scheme to solve their numerical solutions. This simplified equation has elegant and neat structure, which is more convenient for both theoretical analysis and numerical computation.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the simplified eigen equation structure derived for nonlinear spinor fields coupled to interaction fields will reveal a discrete energy spectrum with characteristic scaling relationships between coupling constants and eigenvalue magnitudes that can serve as predictive signatures distinguishing different classes of elementary particle models, testable through systematic numerical parameter sweeps comparing convergence rates and spectral properties against known experimental particle mass ratios.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the simplified eigen equation structure derived for nonlinear spinor fields coupled to interaction fields will reveal a discrete energy spectrum with characteristic scaling relationships between coupling constants and eigenvalue magnitudes that can serve as predictive signatures distinguishing different classes of elementary particle models, testable through systematic numerical parameter sweeps comparing convergence rates and spectral properties against known experimental particle mass ratios.

abstract: Coulomb interaction turns anyonic quasiparticles of a primary quantum Hall liquid with filling factor $\nu =1/(2m+1)$ into hard-core anyons. We have developed a model of coherent transport of such quasiparticles in systems of multiple antidots by extending the Wigner-Jordan description of 1D abelian anyons to tunneling problems. We show that the anyonic exchange statistics manifests itself in tunneling conductance even in the {\em absence} of quasiparticle exchanges. In particular, it can be seen as a non-vanishing resonant peak associated with quasiparticle tunneling through a line of three antidots.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that in a system of four or more antidots arranged linearly, the anyonic exchange statistics of fractional quantum Hall quasiparticles will produce a characteristic oscillatory pattern in the tunneling conductance as a function of gate voltage, with a periodicity and phase structure that uniquely encodes the fractional statistics parameter θ = π/(2m+1), thereby providing a direct spectroscopic fingerprint distinguishable from both fermionic and bosonic transport signatures.
raw_output: We hypothesize that in a system of four or more antidots arranged linearly, the anyonic exchange statistics of fractional quantum Hall quasiparticles will produce a characteristic oscillatory pattern in the tunneling conductance as a function of gate voltage, with a periodicity and phase structure that uniquely encodes the fractional statistics parameter θ = π/(2m+1), thereby providing a direct spectroscopic fingerprint distinguishable from both fermionic and bosonic transport signatures.

abstract: We discuss dynamics of a slow quantum phase transition in a spin-1 Bose-Einstein condensate. We determine analytically the scaling properties of the system magnetization and verify them with numerical simulations in a one dimensional model.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the universal scaling exponents governing magnetization dynamics during a slow quantum phase transition in a spin-1 Bose-Einstein condensate will exhibit a measurable dimensional crossover when the system transitions from a quasi-one-dimensional geometry to a two-dimensional trap configuration, deviating from Kibble-Zurek predictions in a manner that depends quantitatively on the ratio of transverse to longitudinal trapping frequencies.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the universal scaling exponents governing magnetization dynamics during a slow quantum phase transition in a spin-1 Bose-Einstein condensate will exhibit a measurable dimensional crossover when the system transitions from a quasi-one-dimensional geometry to a two-dimensional trap configuration, deviating from Kibble-Zurek predictions in a manner that depends quantitatively on the ratio of transverse to longitudinal trapping frequencies.

abstract: We show that two distinct quantum states of the electromagnetic field can be associated to a classical vector X wave or a propagation-invariant solution of Maxwell equations. The difference between the two states is of pure quantum mechanical origin since they are internally entangled and disentangled, respectively and can be generated by different linear or nonlinear processes. Detection and generation of Schr\"odinger-cat states comprising two entangled X-waves and their possible applications are discussed.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the degree of quantum entanglement between the two X-wave quantum states can be continuously tuned by engineering the phase-matching conditions in a nonlinear optical medium, such that a measurable transition from a separable to a maximally entangled X-wave Schrödinger-cat state can be experimentally observed through coincidence-based Hong-Ou-Mandel interferometry, providing a controllable platform for encoding quantum information in the spatiotemporal degrees of freedom of propagation-invariant photon pairs.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the degree of quantum entanglement between the two X-wave quantum states can be continuously tuned by engineering the phase-matching conditions in a nonlinear optical medium, such that a measurable transition from a separable to a maximally entangled X-wave Schrödinger-cat state can be experimentally observed through coincidence-based Hong-Ou-Mandel interferometry, providing a controllable platform for encoding quantum information in the spatiotemporal degrees of freedom of propagation-invariant photon pairs.

abstract: A rotating, two-component Bose-Einstein condensate is shown to exhibit vortices of multiple quantization, which are possible due to the interatomic interactions between the two species. Also, persistent currents are absent in this system. Finally, the order parameter has a very simple structure for a range of angular momenta.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that in a three-component rotating Bose-Einstein condensate with asymmetric interspecies interaction strengths, the maximum stable vortex quantization number will scale nonlinearly with the number of components, and the introduction of a third species will restore the possibility of persistent currents under specific ratios of intraspecies to interspecies coupling constants.
raw_output: We hypothesize that in a three-component rotating Bose-Einstein condensate with asymmetric interspecies interaction strengths, the maximum stable vortex quantization number will scale nonlinearly with the number of components, and the introduction of a third species will restore the possibility of persistent currents under specific ratios of intraspecies to interspecies coupling constants.

abstract: Correlation-driven screening of disorder is studied within the typical-medium dynamical mean-field theory (TMT-DMFT) of the Mott-Anderson transition. In the strongly correlated regime, the site energies epsilon_R^i characterizing the effective disorder potential are strongly renormalized due to the phenomenon of Kondo pinning. This effect produces very strong screening when the interaction U is stronger then disorder W, but applies only to a fraction of the sites in the opposite limit (U<W).
hypothesis: We hypothesize that in the crossover regime where interaction strength U and disorder W are comparable (U ≈ W), the fraction of Kondo-pinned sites exhibits a non-monotonic dependence on temperature, such that a characteristic temperature scale T* exists below which the screened fraction saturates and above which thermal fluctuations progressively destroy the correlation-driven screening, leading to a measurable enhancement of effective disorder and a corresponding shift in the Mott-Anderson phase boundary.
raw_output: We hypothesize that in the crossover regime where interaction strength U and disorder W are comparable (U ≈ W), the fraction of Kondo-pinned sites exhibits a non-monotonic dependence on temperature, such that a characteristic temperature scale T* exists below which the screened fraction saturates and above which thermal fluctuations progressively destroy the correlation-driven screening, leading to a measurable enhancement of effective disorder and a corresponding shift in the Mott-Anderson phase boundary.

abstract: Single-molecule detection with chemical specificity is a powerful and much desired tool for biology, chemistry, physics, and sensing technologies. Surface-enhanced spectroscopies enable single molecule studies, yet reliable substrates of adequate sensitivity are in short supply. We present a simple, scaleable substrate for surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) incorporating nanometer-scale electromigrated gaps between extended electrodes. Molecules in the nanogap active regions exhibit hallmarks of very high Raman sensitivity, including blinking and spectral diffusion. Electrodynamic simulations show plasmonic focusing, giving electromagnetic enhancements approaching those needed for single-molecule SERS.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying a controlled DC bias voltage across the electromigrated nanogap electrodes during SERS measurements will enable active tuning of the plasmonic resonance frequency by modifying the gap geometry through controlled atomic-scale electromigration, thereby allowing optimization of electromagnetic enhancement for specific target molecule Raman signatures in real time.
raw_output: We hypothesize that applying a controlled DC bias voltage across the electromigrated nanogap electrodes during SERS measurements will enable active tuning of the plasmonic resonance frequency by modifying the gap geometry through controlled atomic-scale electromigration, thereby allowing optimization of electromagnetic enhancement for specific target molecule Raman signatures in real time.

abstract: We have performed a systematic angle-resolved photoemission study of as-grown and oxygen-reduced Pr$_{2-x}$Ce$_x$CuO$_4$ and Pr$_{1-x}$LaCe$_{x}$CuO$_4$ electron-doped cuprates. In contrast to the common belief, neither the band filling nor the band parameters are significantly affected by the oxygen reduction process. Instead, we show that the main electronic role of the reduction process is to remove an anisotropic leading edge gap around the Fermi surface. While the nodal leading edge gap is induced by long-range antiferomagnetic order, the origin of the antinodal one remains unclear.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the antinodal leading edge gap observed in as-grown electron-doped cuprates prior to oxygen reduction originates from short-range charge or orbital ordering associated with apical oxygen defects, which locally break the CuO₂ plane symmetry and can be distinguished from antiferromagnetic contributions by systematic neutron scattering and resonant X-ray scattering measurements as a function of controlled apical oxygen stoichiometry.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the antinodal leading edge gap observed in as-grown electron-doped cuprates prior to oxygen reduction originates from short-range charge or orbital ordering associated with apical oxygen defects, which locally break the CuO₂ plane symmetry and can be distinguished from antiferromagnetic contributions by systematic neutron scattering and resonant X-ray scattering measurements as a function of controlled apical oxygen stoichiometry.

abstract: We provide a quantum analysis of a DC SQUID mechanical displacement detector within the sub-critical Josephson current regime. A segment of the SQUID loop forms the mechanical resonator and motion of the latter is transduced inductively through changes in the flux threading the loop. Expressions are derived for the detector signal response and noise, which are used to evaluate the position and force detection sensitivity. We also investigate cooling of the mechanical resonator due to back reaction noise from the detector.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that by engineering the geometric asymmetry of the DC SQUID loop such that the mechanical resonator segment occupies a precisely tuned fraction of the total loop inductance, it is possible to simultaneously optimize both the position detection sensitivity and the back-action cooling rate, reaching a regime where the mechanical resonator can be driven to its quantum ground state at temperatures significantly higher than those achievable with a symmetric loop configuration.
raw_output: We hypothesize that by engineering the geometric asymmetry of the DC SQUID loop such that the mechanical resonator segment occupies a precisely tuned fraction of the total loop inductance, it is possible to simultaneously optimize both the position detection sensitivity and the back-action cooling rate, reaching a regime where the mechanical resonator can be driven to its quantum ground state at temperatures significantly higher than those achievable with a symmetric loop configuration.

abstract: Annealed importance sampling is a means to assign equilibrium weights to a nonequilibrium sample that was generated by a simulated annealing protocol. The weights may then be used to calculate equilibrium averages, and also serve as an ``adiabatic signature'' of the chosen cooling schedule. In this paper we demonstrate the method on the 50-atom dileucine peptide, showing that equilibrium distributions are attained for manageable cooling schedules. For this system, as naively implemented here, the method is modestly more efficient than constant temperature simulation. However, the method is worth considering whenever any simulated heating or cooling is performed (as is often done at the beginning of a simulation project, or during an NMR structure calculation), as it is simple to implement and requires minimal additional CPU expense. Furthermore, the naive implementation presented here can be improved.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating adaptive cooling schedules—where the annealing rate is dynamically adjusted based on real-time monitoring of the adiabatic signature (importance weights) to maintain a target effective sample size—will substantially improve the efficiency of annealed importance sampling for larger peptide systems compared to the fixed cooling schedules demonstrated for dileucine, yielding more accurate equilibrium distributions with fewer total computational steps.
raw_output: We hypothesize that incorporating adaptive cooling schedules—where the annealing rate is dynamically adjusted based on real-time monitoring of the adiabatic signature (importance weights) to maintain a target effective sample size—will substantially improve the efficiency of annealed importance sampling for larger peptide systems compared to the fixed cooling schedules demonstrated for dileucine, yielding more accurate equilibrium distributions with fewer total computational steps.

abstract: A recently introduced nonlinear Fokker-Planck equation, derived directly from a master equation, comes out as a very general tool to describe phenomenologically systems presenting complex behavior, like anomalous diffusion, in the presence of external forces. Such an equation is characterized by a nonlinear diffusion term that may present, in general, two distinct powers of the probability distribution. Herein, we calculate the stationary-state distributions of this equation in some special cases, and introduce associated classes of generalized entropies in order to satisfy the H-theorem. Within this approach, the parameters associated with the transition rates of the original master-equation are related to such generalized entropies, and are shown to obey some restrictions. Some particular cases are discussed.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the generalized entropy classes derived from the nonlinear Fokker-Planck equation with two distinct diffusion power exponents can be unified under a single parameterized entropic functional whose optimization, subject to appropriate constraints, yields stationary-state distributions that continuously interpolate between Tsallis, Boltzmann-Gibbs, and other known non-extensive statistics as the two power exponents are systematically varied, thereby establishing a universal phase diagram in parameter space that demarcates distinct universality classes of anomalous diffusion.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the generalized entropy classes derived from the nonlinear Fokker-Planck equation with two distinct diffusion power exponents can be unified under a single parameterized entropic functional whose optimization, subject to appropriate constraints, yields stationary-state distributions that continuously interpolate between Tsallis, Boltzmann-Gibbs, and other known non-extensive statistics as the two power exponents are systematically varied, thereby establishing a universal phase diagram in parameter space that demarcates distinct universality classes of anomalous diffusion.

abstract: The spin-lattice relaxation time, $T_{1}$, of conduction electrons is measured as a function of temperature and magnetic field in MgB$_2$. The method is based on the detection of the $z$ component of the conduction electron magnetization under electron spin resonance conditions with amplitude modulated microwave excitation. Measurement of $T_{1}$ below $T_c$ at 0.32 T allows to disentangle contributions from the two Fermi surfaces of MgB$_{2}$ as this field restores normal state on the Fermi surface part with $\pi$ symmetry only.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying a magnetic field precisely tuned to selectively suppress superconductivity on the π-band Fermi surface of MgB₂ will reveal a measurable anisotropy in the spin-lattice relaxation time T₁ between the π and σ bands that scales with the ratio of their respective electron-phonon coupling constants, providing a direct experimental probe of inter-band scattering contributions to spin relaxation in multiband superconductors.
raw_output: We hypothesize that applying a magnetic field precisely tuned to selectively suppress superconductivity on the π-band Fermi surface of MgB₂ will reveal a measurable anisotropy in the spin-lattice relaxation time T₁ between the π and σ bands that scales with the ratio of their respective electron-phonon coupling constants, providing a direct experimental probe of inter-band scattering contributions to spin relaxation in multiband superconductors.

abstract: We introduce a simple nearest-neighbor spin model with multiple metastable phases, the number and decay pathways of which are explicitly controlled by the parameters of the system. With this model we can construct, for example, a system which evolves through an arbitrarily long succession of metastable phases. We also construct systems in which different phases may nucleate competitively from a single initial phase. For such a system, we present a general method to extract from numerical simulations the individual nucleation rates of the nucleating phases. The results show that the Ostwald rule, which predicts which phase will nucleate, must be modified probabilistically when the new phases are almost equally stable. Finally, we show that the nucleation rate of a phase depends, among other things, on the number of other phases accessible from it.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that in competitive multi-phase nucleation systems where the Ostwald rule breaks down due to near-equal phase stabilities, the probability distribution governing which phase nucleates first follows a relationship that is quantitatively predictable from the ratio of individual nucleation rates, such that the crossover from deterministic Ostwald-rule behavior to probabilistic nucleation can be characterized by a dimensionless parameter defined by the difference in free energy barriers relative to thermal energy, with a universal threshold value below which stochastic effects dominate regardless of the specific spin model parameters.
raw_output: We hypothesize that in competitive multi-phase nucleation systems where the Ostwald rule breaks down due to near-equal phase stabilities, the probability distribution governing which phase nucleates first follows a relationship that is quantitatively predictable from the ratio of individual nucleation rates, such that the crossover from deterministic Ostwald-rule behavior to probabilistic nucleation can be characterized by a dimensionless parameter defined by the difference in free energy barriers relative to thermal energy, with a universal threshold value below which stochastic effects dominate regardless of the specific spin model parameters.

abstract: We present results illustrating the effects of using explicit summation terms for the $r^{-6}$ dispersion term on the interfacial properties of a Lennard-Jones fluid and SPC/E water. For the Lennard-Jones fluid, we find that the use of long-range summations, even with a short ``crossover radius,'' yields results that are consistent with simulations using large cutoff radii. Simulations of SPC/E water demonstrate that the long-range dispersion forces are of secondary importance to the Coulombic forces. In both cases, we find that the ratio of box size $L_{\parallel}$ to crossover radius $r_{\rm c}^{\mathbf k}$ plays an important role in determining the magnitude of the long-range dispersion correction, although its effect is secondary when Coulombic interactions are also present.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that for polar molecular fluids with significant quadrupole moments (such as CO₂ or N₂), the relative importance of long-range dispersion corrections to interfacial properties will occupy an intermediate regime between that of the Lennard-Jones fluid and SPC/E water, with a quantifiable crossover radius threshold below which surface tension errors exceed 5% that scales predictably with the ratio of electrostatic to dispersive interaction strengths.
raw_output: We hypothesize that for polar molecular fluids with significant quadrupole moments (such as CO₂ or N₂), the relative importance of long-range dispersion corrections to interfacial properties will occupy an intermediate regime between that of the Lennard-Jones fluid and SPC/E water, with a quantifiable crossover radius threshold below which surface tension errors exceed 5% that scales predictably with the ratio of electrostatic to dispersive interaction strengths.

abstract: We investigate the vortex state with Fulde-Ferrell-Larkin-Ovchinnikov (FFLO) modulations suggested for a high field phase of CeCoIn_5. On the basis of the quasiclassical Eilenberger theory, we calculate the three dimensional structure of pair potentials, internal magnetic fields, paramagnetic moments, and electronic states, for the s-wave and the d-wave pairings comparatively. The \pi-phase shift of the pair potential at the FFLO nodal plane or at the vortex core induces sharp peak states in the local density of states, and enhances the local paramagnetic moment. We also discuss the NMR spectrum and the neutron scattering as methods to detect the FFLO structure.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the sharp peak states in the local density of states arising from π-phase shifts at FFLO nodal planes will produce a distinctive thermal conductivity anisotropy in CeCoIn₅ that is qualitatively different from conventional vortex states, such that heat transport parallel versus perpendicular to the FFLO modulation wavevector will exhibit a characteristic crossover temperature that scales with the FFLO modulation wavelength and can be experimentally distinguished from d-wave gap node contributions through systematic angular-dependent measurements under rotating magnetic fields.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the sharp peak states in the local density of states arising from π-phase shifts at FFLO nodal planes will produce a distinctive thermal conductivity anisotropy in CeCoIn₅ that is qualitatively different from conventional vortex states, such that heat transport parallel versus perpendicular to the FFLO modulation wavevector will exhibit a characteristic crossover temperature that scales with the FFLO modulation wavelength and can be experimentally distinguished from d-wave gap node contributions through systematic angular-dependent measurements under rotating magnetic fields.

abstract: We measured the correlation of the times between successive flaps of a flag for a variety of wind speeds and found no evidence of low dimensional chaotic behavior in the return maps of these times. We instead observed what is best modeled as random times determined by an exponential distribution. This study was done as an undergraduate experiment and illustrates the differences between low dimensional chaotic and possibly higher dimensional chaotic systems.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the exponential distribution of inter-flap times observed in flag flapping will transition to a non-exponential (e.g., power-law or bimodal) distribution at critical wind speeds corresponding to Reynolds number thresholds where coherent vortex shedding becomes dominant, revealing an underlying deterministic structure that emerges only within specific fluid-dynamic regimes.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the exponential distribution of inter-flap times observed in flag flapping will transition to a non-exponential (e.g., power-law or bimodal) distribution at critical wind speeds corresponding to Reynolds number thresholds where coherent vortex shedding becomes dominant, revealing an underlying deterministic structure that emerges only within specific fluid-dynamic regimes.

abstract: Because observations of galaxies and clusters have been found inconsistent with General Relativity (GR), the focus of effort in developing a Scalar Potential Model (SPM) has been on the examination of galaxies and clusters. The SPM has been found to be consistent with cluster cellular structure, the flow of IGM from spiral galaxies to elliptical galaxies, intergalactic redshift without an expanding universe, discrete redshift, rotation curve (RC) data without dark matter, asymmetric RCs, galaxy central mass, galaxy central velocity dispersion, and the Pioneer Anomaly. In addition, the SPM suggests a model of past expansion, past contraction, and current expansion of the universe. GR corresponds to the SPM in the limit in which the effect of the Sources and Sinks approximate a flat scalar potential field such as between clusters and on the solar system scale, which is small relative to the distance to a Source.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Scalar Potential Model's Sources and Sinks framework predicts a measurable, spatially anisotropic redshift signal in the intergalactic medium at the boundaries between adjacent cluster cells that is systematically distinct from Hubble flow redshift, such that spectroscopic surveys of galaxy filaments at cluster-cluster interfaces should reveal discrete redshift discontinuities whose magnitude scales predictably with the distance from the nearest Source or Sink, providing a falsifiable observational test that distinguishes SPM from both standard ΛCDM cosmology and General Relativity.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the Scalar Potential Model's Sources and Sinks framework predicts a measurable, spatially anisotropic redshift signal in the intergalactic medium at the boundaries between adjacent cluster cells that is systematically distinct from Hubble flow redshift, such that spectroscopic surveys of galaxy filaments at cluster-cluster interfaces should reveal discrete redshift discontinuities whose magnitude scales predictably with the distance from the nearest Source or Sink, providing a falsifiable observational test that distinguishes SPM from both standard ΛCDM cosmology and General Relativity.

abstract: We present an analysis of the percolation transition for general node removal strategies valid for locally tree-like directed networks. On the basis of heuristic arguments we predict that, if the probability of removing node $i$ is $p_i$, the network disintegrates if $p_i$ is such that the largest eigenvalue of the matrix with entries $A_{ij}(1-p_i)$ is less than 1, where $A$ is the adjacency matrix of the network. The knowledge or applicability of a Markov network model is not required by our theory, thus making it applicable to situations not covered by previous works. We test our predicted percolation criterion against numerical results for different networks and node removal strategies.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the eigenvalue-based percolation criterion derived for node removal strategies can be extended to edge-targeted attack strategies on locally tree-like directed networks, such that network disintegration occurs when the largest eigenvalue of the matrix with entries $A_{ij}(1-q_{ij})$ falls below 1, where $q_{ij}$ represents the heterogeneous probability of removing directed edge $(i,j)$, thereby unifying node and edge percolation transitions within a single spectral framework.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the eigenvalue-based percolation criterion derived for node removal strategies can be extended to edge-targeted attack strategies on locally tree-like directed networks, such that network disintegration occurs when the largest eigenvalue of the matrix with entries $A_{ij}(1-q_{ij})$ falls below 1, where $q_{ij}$ represents the heterogeneous probability of removing directed edge $(i,j)$, thereby unifying node and edge percolation transitions within a single spectral framework.

abstract: We find three distinct phases; a tubular phase, a planar phase, and the spherical phase, in a triangulated fluid surface model. It is also found that these phases are separated by discontinuous transitions. The fluid surface model is investigated within the framework of the conventional curvature model by using the canonical Monte Carlo simulations with dynamical triangulations. The mechanical strength of the surface is given only by skeletons, and no two-dimensional bending energy is assumed in the Hamiltonian. The skeletons are composed of elastic linear-chains and rigid junctions and form a compartmentalized structure on the surface, and for this reason the vertices of triangles can diffuse freely only inside the compartments. As a consequence, an inhomogeneous structure is introduced in the model; the surface strength inside the compartments is different from the surface strength on the compartments. However, the rotational symmetry is not influenced by the elastic skeletons; there is no specific direction on the surface. In addition to the three phases mentioned above, a collapsed phase is expected to exist in the low bending rigidity regime that was not studied here. The inhomogeneous structure and the fluidity of vertices are considered to be the origin of such variety of phases.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematically varying the ratio of compartment size to total surface area in the triangulated fluid surface model will reveal a critical compartment size threshold below which the discontinuous tubular-to-planar and planar-to-spherical phase transitions shift to continuous transitions, due to the suppression of long-range membrane undulations when compartment dimensions approach the persistence length of the skeleton network.
raw_output: We hypothesize that systematically varying the ratio of compartment size to total surface area in the triangulated fluid surface model will reveal a critical compartment size threshold below which the discontinuous tubular-to-planar and planar-to-spherical phase transitions shift to continuous transitions, due to the suppression of long-range membrane undulations when compartment dimensions approach the persistence length of the skeleton network.

abstract: The effect of fractal normal-phase clusters on vortex dynamics in a percolative superconductor is considered. The superconductor contains percolative superconducting cluster carrying a transport current and clusters of a normal phase, acting as pinning centers. A prototype of such a structure is YBCO film, containing clusters of columnar defects, as well as the BSCCO/Ag sheathed tape, which is of practical interest for wire fabrication. Transition of the superconductor into a resistive state corresponds to the percolation transition from a pinned vortex state to a resistive state when the vortices are free to move. The dependencies of the free vortex density on the fractal dimension of the cluster boundary as well as the resistance on the transport current are obtained. It is revealed that a mixed state of the vortex glass type is realized in the superconducting system involved. The current-voltage characteristics of superconductors containing fractal clusters are obtained and their features are studied.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that increasing the fractal dimension of normal-phase cluster boundaries beyond a critical threshold will produce a non-monotonic dependence of the critical current density on fractal dimension, whereby highly irregular cluster boundaries initially enhance vortex pinning efficiency but ultimately reduce it by creating percolative pathways that facilitate collective vortex motion, a relationship that can be empirically tested by systematically varying ion irradiation parameters in YBCO films to generate columnar defect clusters with tunable fractal boundary dimensions and measuring the resulting current-voltage characteristics.
raw_output: We hypothesize that increasing the fractal dimension of normal-phase cluster boundaries beyond a critical threshold will produce a non-monotonic dependence of the critical current density on fractal dimension, whereby highly irregular cluster boundaries initially enhance vortex pinning efficiency but ultimately reduce it by creating percolative pathways that facilitate collective vortex motion, a relationship that can be empirically tested by systematically varying ion irradiation parameters in YBCO films to generate columnar defect clusters with tunable fractal boundary dimensions and measuring the resulting current-voltage characteristics.

abstract: The nature of quantum criticality in CeCoIn$_5$ is studied by low-temperature thermal expansion $\alpha(T)$. At the field-induced quantum critical point at H=5 T a crossover scale $T^\star\approx 0.3$ K is observed, separating $\alpha(T)/T\propto T^{-1}$ from a weaker $T^{-1/2}$ divergence. We ascribe this change to a crossover in the dimensionality of the critical fluctuations which may be coupled to a change from unconventional to conventional quantum criticality. Disorder, whose effect on quantum criticality is studied in CeCoIn$_{5-x}$Sn$_x$ ($0\leq x\leq 0.18$), shifts $T^\star$ towards higher temperatures.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the crossover temperature T★ in CeCoIn₅ exhibits an anisotropic response to uniaxial pressure applied along different crystallographic axes, with pressure along the c-axis selectively suppressing the low-temperature T⁻¹/² divergence regime by stabilizing three-dimensional critical fluctuations, thereby providing a means to continuously tune the dimensionality crossover independently of disorder or magnetic field.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the crossover temperature T★ in CeCoIn₅ exhibits an anisotropic response to uniaxial pressure applied along different crystallographic axes, with pressure along the c-axis selectively suppressing the low-temperature T⁻¹/² divergence regime by stabilizing three-dimensional critical fluctuations, thereby providing a means to continuously tune the dimensionality crossover independently of disorder or magnetic field.

abstract: We analyze a probabilistic cellular automaton describing the dynamics of coexistence of a predator-prey system. The individuals of each species are localized over the sites of a lattice and the local stochastic updating rules are inspired on the processes of the Lotka-Volterra model. Two levels of mean-field approximations are set up. The simple approximation is equivalent to an extended patch model, a simple metapopulation model with patches colonized by prey, patches colonized by predators and empty patches. This approximation is capable of describing the limited available space for species occupancy. The pair approximation is moreover able to describe two types of coexistence of prey and predators: one where population densities are constant in time and another displaying self-sustained time-oscillations of the population densities. The oscillations are associated with limit cycles and arise through a Hopf bifurcation. They are stable against changes in the initial conditions and, in this sense, they differ from the Lotka-Volterra cycles which depend on initial conditions. In this respect, the present model is biologically more realistic than the Lotka-Volterra model.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spatial correlation length of predator-prey clusters on the lattice serves as a quantitative predictor of proximity to the Hopf bifurcation point, such that as the system approaches the critical parameter values separating steady-state coexistence from limit-cycle oscillations, the spatial correlations between neighboring predator and prey pairs will diverge in a manner analogous to critical slowing down, providing an empirically measurable early-warning signal of the transition between dynamical regimes.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the spatial correlation length of predator-prey clusters on the lattice serves as a quantitative predictor of proximity to the Hopf bifurcation point, such that as the system approaches the critical parameter values separating steady-state coexistence from limit-cycle oscillations, the spatial correlations between neighboring predator and prey pairs will diverge in a manner analogous to critical slowing down, providing an empirically measurable early-warning signal of the transition between dynamical regimes.

abstract: We evaluate the Coulomb drag current in two finite-length Tomonaga-Luttinger-liquid wires coupled by an electrostatic backscattering interaction. The drag current in one wire shows oscillations as a function of the bias voltage applied to the other wire, reflecting interferences of the plasmon standing waves in the interacting wires. In agreement with this picture, the amplitude of the current oscillations is reduced with increasing temperature. This is a clear signature of non-Fermi-liquid physics because for coupled Fermi liquids the drag resistance is always expected to increase as the temperature is raised.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that in asymmetric coupled Tomonaga-Luttinger-liquid wires of unequal lengths, the interference pattern of the Coulomb drag current oscillations as a function of bias voltage will exhibit a beating phenomenon arising from the incommensurability of the two distinct plasmon standing wave frequencies, with the beating frequency directly proportional to the ratio of wire lengths and providing a measurable experimental signature that uniquely distinguishes non-Fermi-liquid behavior from any classical drag mechanism.
raw_output: We hypothesize that in asymmetric coupled Tomonaga-Luttinger-liquid wires of unequal lengths, the interference pattern of the Coulomb drag current oscillations as a function of bias voltage will exhibit a beating phenomenon arising from the incommensurability of the two distinct plasmon standing wave frequencies, with the beating frequency directly proportional to the ratio of wire lengths and providing a measurable experimental signature that uniquely distinguishes non-Fermi-liquid behavior from any classical drag mechanism.

abstract: In this paper we investigate some entanglement properties for the Hydrogen molecule considered as a two interacting spin 1/2 (qubit) model. The entanglement related to the $H_{2}$ molecule is evaluated both using the von Neumann entropy and the Concurrence and it is compared with the corresponding quantities for the two interacting spin system. Many aspects of these functions are examinated employing in part analytical and, essentially, numerical techniques. We have compared analogous results obtained by Huang and Kais a few years ago. In this respect, some possible controversial situations are presented and discussed.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the discrepancies in entanglement measures (von Neumann entropy and Concurrence) between the H₂ molecule and the idealized two interacting spin-1/2 model become systematically amplified at internuclear distances near the dissociation limit, where electron correlation effects dominate, such that incorporating higher-order configuration interaction corrections into the two-qubit Hamiltonian will yield a critical coupling parameter threshold beyond which the concurrence exhibits a non-analytic transition that mirrors the quantum phase transition behavior observed in the full molecular system.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the discrepancies in entanglement measures (von Neumann entropy and Concurrence) between the H₂ molecule and the idealized two interacting spin-1/2 model become systematically amplified at internuclear distances near the dissociation limit, where electron correlation effects dominate, such that incorporating higher-order configuration interaction corrections into the two-qubit Hamiltonian will yield a critical coupling parameter threshold beyond which the concurrence exhibits a non-analytic transition that mirrors the quantum phase transition behavior observed in the full molecular system.

abstract: Systems of strongly correlated fermions on certain geometrically frustrated lattices at particular filling factors support excitations with fractional charges $\pm e/2$. We calculate quantum mechanical ground states, low--lying excitations and spectral functions of finite lattices by means of numerical diagonalization. The ground state of the most thoroughfully studied case, the criss-crossed checkerboard lattice, is degenerate and shows long--range order. Static fractional charges are confined by a weak linear force, most probably leading to bound states of large spatial extent. Consequently, the quasi-particle weight is reduced, which reflects the internal dynamics of the fractionally charged excitations. By using an additional parameter, we fine--tune the system to a special point at which fractional charges are manifestly deconfined--the so--called Rokhsar--Kivelson point. For a deeper understanding of the low--energy physics of these models and for numerical advantages, several conserved quantum numbers are identified.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the crossover from confinement to deconfinement of fractional charges in the criss-crossed checkerboard lattice system can be characterized by a universal scaling relationship between the confining string tension and the spatial extent of the bound states, such that tuning an additional coupling parameter continuously toward the Rokhsar-Kivelson point will produce a divergence in the bound-state size following a power-law scaling consistent with a second-order quantum phase transition, detectable as a singular feature in the finite-size scaling of the quasi-particle spectral weight.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the crossover from confinement to deconfinement of fractional charges in the criss-crossed checkerboard lattice system can be characterized by a universal scaling relationship between the confining string tension and the spatial extent of the bound states, such that tuning an additional coupling parameter continuously toward the Rokhsar-Kivelson point will produce a divergence in the bound-state size following a power-law scaling consistent with a second-order quantum phase transition, detectable as a singular feature in the finite-size scaling of the quasi-particle spectral weight.

abstract: We performed the first scanning tunneling spectroscopy measurements on the pyrochlore superconductor KOs2O6 (Tc = 9.6 K) in both zero magnetic field and the vortex state at several temperatures above 1.95 K. This material presents atomically flat surfaces, yielding spatially homogeneous spectra which reveal fully-gapped superconductivity with a gap anisotropy of 30%. Measurements performed at fields of 2 and 6 T display a hexagonal Abrikosov flux line lattice. From the shape of the vortex cores, we extract a coherence length of 31-40 {\AA}, in agreement with the value derived from the upper critical field Hc2. We observe a reduction in size of the vortex cores (and hence the coherence length) with increasing field which is consistent with the unexpectedly high and unsaturated upper critical field reported.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the field-dependent reduction in vortex core size observed in KOs2O6 is driven by a Fermi surface reconstruction induced by orbital effects at high magnetic fields, which progressively suppresses the effective quasiparticle density of states within the vortex cores and can be directly verified by field-dependent scanning tunneling spectroscopy measurements showing a systematic shift in the zero-bias conductance peak intensity as a function of applied field up to Hc2.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the field-dependent reduction in vortex core size observed in KOs2O6 is driven by a Fermi surface reconstruction induced by orbital effects at high magnetic fields, which progressively suppresses the effective quasiparticle density of states within the vortex cores and can be directly verified by field-dependent scanning tunneling spectroscopy measurements showing a systematic shift in the zero-bias conductance peak intensity as a function of applied field up to Hc2.

abstract: In this paper, we briefly present our work on the role of transition-metal element in electronic structure and transport properties of quasicrystals and related complex phases. Several Parts of these works have been done or initiated in collaboration with Prof. T. Fujiwara.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the systematic substitution of transition-metal elements with varying d-orbital filling across the 3d series in icosahedral quasicrystals will produce a non-monotonic relationship between electron concentration and electrical conductivity, with specific d-electron counts corresponding to pseudogap features at the Fermi level that can be predicted by a generalized Hume-Rothery stabilization mechanism quantifiable through ab initio electronic structure calculations.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the systematic substitution of transition-metal elements with varying d-orbital filling across the 3d series in icosahedral quasicrystals will produce a non-monotonic relationship between electron concentration and electrical conductivity, with specific d-electron counts corresponding to pseudogap features at the Fermi level that can be predicted by a generalized Hume-Rothery stabilization mechanism quantifiable through ab initio electronic structure calculations.

abstract: Comprehensive x-ray scattering studies, including resonant scattering at Mn L-edge, Tb L- and M-edges, were performed on single crystals of TbMn2O5. X-ray intensities were observed at a forbidden Bragg position in the ferroelectric phases, in addition to the lattice and the magnetic modulation peaks. Temperature dependences of their intensities and the relation between the modulation wave vectors provide direct evidences of exchange striction induced ferroelectricity. Resonant x-ray scattering results demonstrate the presence of multiple magnetic orders by exhibiting their different temperature dependences. The commensurate-to-incommensurate phase transition around 24 K is attributed to discommensuration through phase slipping of the magnetic orders in spin frustrated geometries. We proposed that the low temperature incommensurate phase consists of the commensurate magnetic domains separated by anti-phase domain walls which reduce spontaneous polarizations abruptly at the transition.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the density and spatial distribution of anti-phase domain walls in the low-temperature incommensurate phase of TbMn2O5 can be systematically tuned by applied electric fields, thereby enabling reversible control of the commensurate-to-incommensurate phase transition temperature and providing a mechanism to recover the suppressed spontaneous polarization through electric-field-driven domain wall annihilation.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the density and spatial distribution of anti-phase domain walls in the low-temperature incommensurate phase of TbMn2O5 can be systematically tuned by applied electric fields, thereby enabling reversible control of the commensurate-to-incommensurate phase transition temperature and providing a mechanism to recover the suppressed spontaneous polarization through electric-field-driven domain wall annihilation.

abstract: A combination of experimental techniques and molecular dynamics (MD) computer simulation is used to investigate the diffusion dynamics in Al80Ni20 melts. Experimentally, the self-diffusion coefficient of Ni is measured by the long-capillary (LC) method and by quasielastic neutron scattering. The LC method yields also the interdiffusion coefficient. Whereas the experiments were done in the normal liquid state, the simulations provided the determination of both self-diffusion and interdiffusion constants in the undercooled regime as well. The simulation results show good agreement with the experimental data. In the temperature range 3000 K >= T >= 715 K, the interdiffusion coefficient is larger than the self-diffusion constants. Furthermore the simulation shows that this difference becomes larger in the undercooled regime. This result can be refered to a relatively strong temperature dependence of the thermodynamic factor \Phi, which describes the thermodynamic driving force for interdiffusion. The simulations also indicate that the Darken equation is a good approximation, even in the undercooled regime. This implies that dynamic cross correlations play a minor role for the temperature range under consideration.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the thermodynamic factor Φ in Al-Ni melts exhibits a composition-dependent divergence from unity that becomes increasingly pronounced as nickel content deviates from the Al₈₀Ni₂₀ composition upon approaching the glass transition temperature, such that alloys near eutectic or intermetallic stoichiometries (e.g., Al₃Ni or AlNi) will display a significantly stronger decoupling between interdiffusion and self-diffusion coefficients in the undercooled regime compared to Al₈₀Ni₂₀, reflecting enhanced thermodynamic driving forces associated with short-range chemical ordering around those compositions.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the thermodynamic factor Φ in Al-Ni melts exhibits a composition-dependent divergence from unity that becomes increasingly pronounced as nickel content deviates from the Al₈₀Ni₂₀ composition upon approaching the glass transition temperature, such that alloys near eutectic or intermetallic stoichiometries (e.g., Al₃Ni or AlNi) will display a significantly stronger decoupling between interdiffusion and self-diffusion coefficients in the undercooled regime compared to Al₈₀Ni₂₀, reflecting enhanced thermodynamic driving forces associated with short-range chemical ordering around those compositions.

abstract: We investigated structure and morphology of PTFE layers deposited by vacuum process in dependence on deposition parameters: deposition rate, deposition temperature, electron activation energy and activation current. Pentacene (PnC) layers deposited on top of those PTFE films are used as a tool to demonstrate the orienting ability of the PTFE layers. The molecular structure of the PTFE films was investigated by use of infrared spectroscopy. By means of ellipsometry, values of refractive index between 1.33 and 1.36 have been obtained for PTFE films in dependence on deposition conditions. Using the cold friction technique orienting PTFE layers with unidirectional grooves are obtained. On top of these PTFE films oriented PnC layers were grown. The obtained order depends both on the PTFE layer thickness and on PnC growth temperature.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the degree of molecular orientation in vacuum-deposited PTFE alignment layers, as quantified by infrared spectroscopy anisotropy, will exhibit a non-linear threshold dependence on film thickness below which pentacene crystallographic ordering collapses abruptly, and that this critical thickness threshold will shift systematically to lower values as the pentacene deposition temperature approaches the substrate's thermal expansion mismatch point with PTFE, reflecting a coupled interplay between PTFE chain conformation density and pentacene nucleation kinetics at the organic–organic interface.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the degree of molecular orientation in vacuum-deposited PTFE alignment layers, as quantified by infrared spectroscopy anisotropy, will exhibit a non-linear threshold dependence on film thickness below which pentacene crystallographic ordering collapses abruptly, and that this critical thickness threshold will shift systematically to lower values as the pentacene deposition temperature approaches the substrate's thermal expansion mismatch point with PTFE, reflecting a coupled interplay between PTFE chain conformation density and pentacene nucleation kinetics at the organic–organic interface.

abstract: According to extensive experimental findings, the Ginzburg temperature $t_{G}$ for ionic fluids differs substantially from that of nonionic fluids [Schr\"oer W., Weig\"{a}rtner H. 2004 {\it Pure Appl. Chem.} {\bf 76} 19]. A theoretical investigation of this outcome is proposed here by a mean field analysis of the interplay of short and long range interactions on the value of $t_{G}$. We consider a quite general continuous charge-asymmetric model made of charged hard spheres with additional short-range interactions (without electrostatic interactions the model belongs to the same universality class as the 3D Ising model). The effective Landau-Ginzburg Hamiltonian of the full system near its gas-liquid critical point is derived from which the Ginzburg temperature is calculated as a function of the ionicity. The results obtained in this way for $t_{G}$ are in good qualitative and sufficient quantitative agreement with available experimental data.
