hypothesis: We hypothesize that in charge-asymmetric ionic fluids, the crossover length scale at which fluctuation corrections to mean-field behavior become significant exhibits a non-monotonic dependence on the degree of charge asymmetry, such that there exists an optimal charge ratio at which the Ginzburg temperature reaches a maximum, a prediction that can be tested by systematic calorimetric and light-scattering measurements on a series of ionic fluids with systematically varied cation-to-anion charge ratios near their gas-liquid critical points.
raw_output: We hypothesize that in charge-asymmetric ionic fluids, the crossover length scale at which fluctuation corrections to mean-field behavior become significant exhibits a non-monotonic dependence on the degree of charge asymmetry, such that there exists an optimal charge ratio at which the Ginzburg temperature reaches a maximum, a prediction that can be tested by systematic calorimetric and light-scattering measurements on a series of ionic fluids with systematically varied cation-to-anion charge ratios near their gas-liquid critical points.

abstract: In response to the comment of Kenzelmann and Harris I show how the continuum theory of spiral multiferroics can be modified to describe general magnetic orders and discuss why the microscopic mechanism of magnetically-induced ferroelectricity usually makes such modifications unnecessary. This explains why the simple model with a single vector order parameter successfully describes thermodynamics and magnetoelectric properties of many spiral multiferroics.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that in spiral multiferroics where competing magnetic interactions give rise to multiple coexisting magnetic order parameters of comparable magnitude, the breakdown of the single-vector-order-parameter approximation will manifest as a measurable anomalous nonlinearity in the magnetoelectric susceptibility tensor at low temperatures, detectable via high-precision polarization measurements under applied magnetic fields oriented perpendicular to the spiral propagation vector.
raw_output: We hypothesize that in spiral multiferroics where competing magnetic interactions give rise to multiple coexisting magnetic order parameters of comparable magnitude, the breakdown of the single-vector-order-parameter approximation will manifest as a measurable anomalous nonlinearity in the magnetoelectric susceptibility tensor at low temperatures, detectable via high-precision polarization measurements under applied magnetic fields oriented perpendicular to the spiral propagation vector.

abstract: Starting from the reduced dynamical model of a two-junction quantum interference device, a quantum analog of the system has been exhibited, in order to extend the well known properties of this device to the quantum regime. By finding eigenvalues of the corresponding Hamiltonian operator, the persistent currents flowing in the ring have been obtained. The resulting quantum analog of the overdamped two-junction quantum interference device can be seen as a supercurrent qubit operating in the limit of negligible capacitance and finite inductance.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that by introducing asymmetry between the two Josephson junctions in the quantum interference device model—through unequal critical currents or junction areas—the resulting modified Hamiltonian will exhibit a tunable energy level splitting in the persistent current spectrum that can be exploited to enhance qubit coherence times beyond those achievable in the symmetric configuration, owing to reduced sensitivity to flux noise at the degeneracy point.
raw_output: We hypothesize that by introducing asymmetry between the two Josephson junctions in the quantum interference device model—through unequal critical currents or junction areas—the resulting modified Hamiltonian will exhibit a tunable energy level splitting in the persistent current spectrum that can be exploited to enhance qubit coherence times beyond those achievable in the symmetric configuration, owing to reduced sensitivity to flux noise at the degeneracy point.

abstract: Useful information about scientific collaboration structures and patterns can be inferred from computer databases of published papers. The genetic programming bibliography is the most complete reference of papers on GP\@. In addition to locating publications, it contains coauthor and coeditor relationships from which a more complete picture of the field emerges. We treat these relationships as undirected small world graphs whose study reveals the community structure of the GP collaborative social network. Automatic analysis discovers new communities and highlights new facets of them. The investigation reveals many similarities between GP and coauthorship networks in other scientific fields but also some subtle differences such as a smaller central network component and a high clustering.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the smaller central network component and higher clustering coefficient observed in the genetic programming collaboration network, compared to other scientific fields, are causally linked to the field's origins from a small number of founding research groups, such that artificially removing the bridging nodes connecting these founding clusters would fragment the GP collaboration network into more isolated sub-communities than equivalent node removal would produce in larger, more distributed scientific collaboration networks.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the smaller central network component and higher clustering coefficient observed in the genetic programming collaboration network, compared to other scientific fields, are causally linked to the field's origins from a small number of founding research groups, such that artificially removing the bridging nodes connecting these founding clusters would fragment the GP collaboration network into more isolated sub-communities than equivalent node removal would produce in larger, more distributed scientific collaboration networks.

abstract: During a crossover via a switching mechanism from one 2-body potential to another as might be applied in modeling (chemical) reactions in the vicinity of bond formation, energy violations would occur due to finite step size which determines the trajectory of the particles relative to the potential interactions of the unbonded state by numerical (e.g. Verlet) integration. This problem is overcome by an algorithm which preserves the coordinates of the system for each move, but corrects for energy discrepancies by ensuring both energy and momentum conservation in the dynamics. The algorithm is tested for a hysteresis loop reaction model with an without the implementation of the algorithm. The tests involve checking the rate of energy flow out of the MD simulation box; in the equilibrium state, no net rate of flows within experimental error should be observed. The temperature and pressure of the box should also be invariant within the range of fluctuation of these quantities. It is demonstrated that the algorithm satisfies these criteria.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the energy-momentum conservation algorithm described for 2-body potential switching can be extended to many-body reactive force fields (such as ReaxFF) involving simultaneous multi-bond formation and breaking events, where its application will reduce artificial thermal drift by a quantifiable margin compared to uncorrected switching schemes, as measured by the net energy flux across the simulation box boundary over extended MD trajectories.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the energy-momentum conservation algorithm described for 2-body potential switching can be extended to many-body reactive force fields (such as ReaxFF) involving simultaneous multi-bond formation and breaking events, where its application will reduce artificial thermal drift by a quantifiable margin compared to uncorrected switching schemes, as measured by the net energy flux across the simulation box boundary over extended MD trajectories.

abstract: An overview is presented of laser spectroscopy experiments with cold, trapped, highly-charged ions, which will be performed at the HITRAP facility at GSI in Darmstadt (Germany). These high-resolution measurements of ground state hyperfine splittings will be three orders of magnitude more precise than previous measurements. Moreover, from a comparison of measurements of the hyperfine splittings in hydrogen- and lithium-like ions of the same isotope, QED effects at high electromagnetic fields can be determined within a few percent. Several candidate ions suited for these laser spectroscopy studies are presented.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that by extending the comparative hyperfine splitting measurements beyond hydrogen- and lithium-like systems to include boron-like ions of the same heavy isotopes at HITRAP, a three-point QED test framework can be established that disentangles nuclear magnetization distribution (Bohr-Weisskopf) effects from pure QED contributions with greater precision than the two-ion comparison method, enabling sub-percent determination of QED corrections in extreme electromagnetic field regimes.
raw_output: We hypothesize that by extending the comparative hyperfine splitting measurements beyond hydrogen- and lithium-like systems to include boron-like ions of the same heavy isotopes at HITRAP, a three-point QED test framework can be established that disentangles nuclear magnetization distribution (Bohr-Weisskopf) effects from pure QED contributions with greater precision than the two-ion comparison method, enabling sub-percent determination of QED corrections in extreme electromagnetic field regimes.

abstract: We study a loop of Josephson junctions that is quenched through its critical temperature. For three or more junctions, symmetry breaking states can be achieved without thermal activation, in spite of the fact that the relaxation time is practically constant when the critical temperature is approached from above. The probability for these states decreases with quenching time, but the dependence is not allometric. For large number of junctions, cooling does not have to be fast. For this case, we evaluate the standard deviation of the induced flux. Our results are consistent with the available experimental data.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that in a loop of Josephson junctions quenched through the critical temperature, the crossover from non-allometric to allometric scaling of symmetry-breaking state probability as a function of quench time occurs at a specific critical junction number that depends on the ratio of the Josephson coupling energy to the thermal energy, such that below this threshold junction number the topological defect formation mechanism is fundamentally distinct from Kibble-Zurek-type dynamics.
raw_output: We hypothesize that in a loop of Josephson junctions quenched through the critical temperature, the crossover from non-allometric to allometric scaling of symmetry-breaking state probability as a function of quench time occurs at a specific critical junction number that depends on the ratio of the Josephson coupling energy to the thermal energy, such that below this threshold junction number the topological defect formation mechanism is fundamentally distinct from Kibble-Zurek-type dynamics.

abstract: A new method, FM-FTS, combining Frequency Modulation heterodyne laser spectroscopy and Fourier Transform Spectroscopy is presented. It provides simultaneous sensitive measurement of absorption and dispersion profiles with broadband spectral coverage capabilities. Experimental demonstration is made on the overtone spectrum of C2H2 in the 1.5 $\mu$m region.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that FM-FTS, when applied to multicomponent gas mixtures containing spectrally overlapping molecular species (such as CH₄ and CO₂ in atmospheric samples), will achieve simultaneous species discrimination and concentration quantification with higher accuracy than conventional FTS alone by exploiting the dispersion profiles as an additional independent spectroscopic observable that breaks the degeneracy between overlapping absorption features.
raw_output: We hypothesize that FM-FTS, when applied to multicomponent gas mixtures containing spectrally overlapping molecular species (such as CH₄ and CO₂ in atmospheric samples), will achieve simultaneous species discrimination and concentration quantification with higher accuracy than conventional FTS alone by exploiting the dispersion profiles as an additional independent spectroscopic observable that breaks the degeneracy between overlapping absorption features.

abstract: In the last years the traditional scenario of Big Bang has been deeply modified by the study of the quantum features of the Universe evolution, proposing again the problem of using local physical laws on cosmic scale, with particular regard to the cosmological constant role. The group extention method shows that the De Sitter group univocally generalizes the Poincare group, formally justifies the cosmological constant use and suggests a new interpretation for Hartle-Hawking boundary conditions in Quantum Cosmology.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the De Sitter group extension framework, by providing a formal justification for the cosmological constant as a geometric group-theoretic invariant rather than a vacuum energy term, predicts a specific quantized spectrum of allowable cosmological constant values that could be empirically distinguished from continuous vacuum energy models through precision measurements of the large-scale structure power spectrum.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the De Sitter group extension framework, by providing a formal justification for the cosmological constant as a geometric group-theoretic invariant rather than a vacuum energy term, predicts a specific quantized spectrum of allowable cosmological constant values that could be empirically distinguished from continuous vacuum energy models through precision measurements of the large-scale structure power spectrum.

abstract: The present work is focused on theoretical and experimental study of localised levels in organic materials suitable for light-emitting devices and field effect transistors by means of thermal techniques. In our work we focused on low molecular compounds as well as on polymers, especially of two classes of materials: oxadiazoles and quinoxalines. Both organic compounds are well know as electron transport materials in OLEDs.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the density and energetic distribution of localized trap states in oxadiazole-based materials, as characterized by thermal techniques, will exhibit a systematic correlation with electron mobility in organic field-effect transistors, such that materials with shallower and narrower trap state distributions will demonstrate superior electron transport performance compared to quinoxaline-based analogues with deeper or broader trap distributions under equivalent device fabrication conditions.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the density and energetic distribution of localized trap states in oxadiazole-based materials, as characterized by thermal techniques, will exhibit a systematic correlation with electron mobility in organic field-effect transistors, such that materials with shallower and narrower trap state distributions will demonstrate superior electron transport performance compared to quinoxaline-based analogues with deeper or broader trap distributions under equivalent device fabrication conditions.

abstract: This work describes a new microfluidic device developed for rapid screening of solubility diagrams. In several parallel channels, hundreds of nanoliter-volume droplets of a given solution are first stored with a gradual variation in the solute concentration. Then, the application of a temperature gradient along these channels enables us to read directly and quantitatively phase diagrams, concentration vs. temperature. We show, using a solution of adipic acid, that we can measure ten points of the solubility curve in less than 1 hr and with only 250 $\mu$L of solution.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the described microfluidic droplet platform can be extended to simultaneously map multicomponent solubility phase diagrams—such as those involving two competing solutes sharing a common solvent—by encoding orthogonal concentration gradients across parallel channel arrays, thereby enabling the rapid determination of ternary phase boundaries (including eutectic and co-crystallization regions) within a single experimental run using less than 500 µL of total solution.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the described microfluidic droplet platform can be extended to simultaneously map multicomponent solubility phase diagrams—such as those involving two competing solutes sharing a common solvent—by encoding orthogonal concentration gradients across parallel channel arrays, thereby enabling the rapid determination of ternary phase boundaries (including eutectic and co-crystallization regions) within a single experimental run using less than 500 µL of total solution.

abstract:
It is known that a subset of fractional quantum Hall wave functions has been expressed as conformal field theory (CFT) correlators, notably the Laughlin wave function at filling factor $
u=1/m$ ($m$ odd) and its quasiholes, and the Pfaffian wave function at $
u=1/2$ and its quasiholes. We develop a general scheme for constructing composite-fermion (CF) wave functions from conformal field theory. Quasiparticles at $
u=1/m$ are created by inserting anyonic vertex operators, $P_{ rac{1}{m}}(z)$, that replace a subset of the electron operators in the correlator. The one-quasiparticle wave function is identical to the corresponding CF wave function, and the two-quasiparticle wave function has correct fractional charge and statistics and is numerically almost identical to the corresponding CF wave function. We further show how to exactly represent the CF wavefunctions in the Jain series $
u = s/(2sp+1)$ as the CFT correlators of a new type of fermionic vertex operators, $V_{p,n}(z)$, constructed from $n$ free compactified bosons; these operators provide the CFT representation of composite fermions carrying $2p$ flux quanta in the $n^{m th}$ CF Landau level. We also construct the corresponding quasiparticle- and quasihole operators and argue that they have the expected fractional charge and statistics. For filling fractions 2/5 and 3/7 we show that the chiral CFTs that describe the bulk wave functions are identical to those given by Wen's general classification of quantum Hall states in terms of $K$-matrices and $l$- and $t$-vectors, and we propose that to be generally true. Our results suggest a general procedure for constructing quasiparticle wave functions for other fractional Hall states, as well as for constructing ground states at filling fractions not contained in the principal Jain series.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the fermionic vertex operators V_{p,n}(z) constructed from n free compactified bosons can be systematically extended to describe non-Abelian fractional quantum Hall states beyond the Jain series—such as the Read-Rezayi states at ν = k/(kM+2)—by incorporating parafermionic CFT sectors alongside the free boson components, yielding composite-fermion wave functions with non-Abelian quasiparticle statistics that are numerically identical to those obtained from exact diagonalization studies.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the fermionic vertex operators V_{p,n}(z) constructed from n free compactified bosons can be systematically extended to describe non-Abelian fractional quantum Hall states beyond the Jain series—such as the Read-Rezayi states at ν = k/(kM+2)—by incorporating parafermionic CFT sectors alongside the free boson components, yielding composite-fermion wave functions with non-Abelian quasiparticle statistics that are numerically identical to those obtained from exact diagonalization studies.

abstract: Thermally stable polymers have attracted a lot of interest due to their potential use as the active component in electronic, optical and optoelectronic applications, such as light-emitting diodes, light emitting electrochemical cells, photodiodes, photovoltaic cells, field effect transistors, optocouplers and optically pumped lasers in solution and solid state.We report results of investigations into the use of thermal treatment of poly(p-phenylene vinylene) (PPV) films grown on a variety of substrates (quartz and glass). Film thickness, morphology and structural properties were investigated by a range of techniques in particular: atomic force microscope - AFM, DEKTAK method, Ellipsometry and UV-VIS spectroscopy.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that controlled thermal annealing of PPV films at temperatures below the degradation threshold will induce a systematic increase in conjugation length and π-π stacking order, resulting in a quantifiable red-shift in UV-VIS absorption maxima and a corresponding improvement in charge carrier mobility that scales predictably with annealing temperature and duration, as measurable by time-of-flight spectroscopy and grazing-incidence X-ray diffraction.
raw_output: We hypothesize that controlled thermal annealing of PPV films at temperatures below the degradation threshold will induce a systematic increase in conjugation length and π-π stacking order, resulting in a quantifiable red-shift in UV-VIS absorption maxima and a corresponding improvement in charge carrier mobility that scales predictably with annealing temperature and duration, as measurable by time-of-flight spectroscopy and grazing-incidence X-ray diffraction.

abstract: We discuss an alternative non-perturbative proof of Bertrand's theorem that leads in a concise way directly to the two allowed fields: the newtonian and the isotropic harmonic oscillator central fields.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the non-perturbative geometric framework underlying this alternative proof of Bertrand's theorem can be systematically extended to higher-dimensional spaces (d > 3), revealing a precise mathematical relationship between the dimensionality of the space and the number of central force laws that permit closed orbits for all bounded trajectories, thereby generalizing Bertrand's theorem to arbitrary Euclidean dimensions.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the non-perturbative geometric framework underlying this alternative proof of Bertrand's theorem can be systematically extended to higher-dimensional spaces (d > 3), revealing a precise mathematical relationship between the dimensionality of the space and the number of central force laws that permit closed orbits for all bounded trajectories, thereby generalizing Bertrand's theorem to arbitrary Euclidean dimensions.

abstract: We use Monte-Carlo simulations to study aging phenomena and the occurence of spinglass phases in systems of single-domain ferromagnetic nanoparticles under the combined influence of dipolar interaction and anisotropy energy, for different combinations of positional and orientational disorder. We find that the magnetic moments oriente themselves preferably parallel to their anisotropy axes and changes of the total magnetization are solely achieved by 180 degree flips of the magnetic moments, as in Ising systems. Since the dipolar interaction favorizes the formation of antiparallel chain-like structures, antiparallel chain-like patterns are frozen in at low temperatures, leading to aging phenomena characteristic for spin-glasses. Contrary to the intuition, these aging effects are more pronounced in ordered than in disordered structures.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that in systems of single-domain ferromagnetic nanoparticles, the counterintuitive enhancement of spin-glass aging effects in ordered versus disordered structures arises because long-range geometric regularity amplifies frustration by enforcing a higher density of competing antiparallel dipolar chain configurations with nearly degenerate energy states, a prediction that can be tested by systematically varying lattice order parameters and measuring the distribution of energy barriers via ac susceptibility and thermoremanent magnetization protocols as a function of structural coherence length.
raw_output: We hypothesize that in systems of single-domain ferromagnetic nanoparticles, the counterintuitive enhancement of spin-glass aging effects in ordered versus disordered structures arises because long-range geometric regularity amplifies frustration by enforcing a higher density of competing antiparallel dipolar chain configurations with nearly degenerate energy states, a prediction that can be tested by systematically varying lattice order parameters and measuring the distribution of energy barriers via ac susceptibility and thermoremanent magnetization protocols as a function of structural coherence length.

abstract: The inter-Landau level transitions observed in far-infrared transmission experiments on few-layer graphene samples show a behaviour characteristic of the linear dispersion expected in graphene. This behaviour persists in relatively thick samples, and is qualitatively different from that of thin samples of bulk graphite.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the crossover thickness at which few-layer graphene's linear Landau level transition behavior transitions to bulk graphite-like quadratic dispersion behavior is determined by interlayer coupling strength and can be precisely identified by measuring the magnetic field dependence of inter-Landau level transition energies as a function of layer number, predicting a critical layer count beyond which the √B scaling of transition energies systematically breaks down in favor of linear-B dependence characteristic of conventional massive fermions.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the crossover thickness at which few-layer graphene's linear Landau level transition behavior transitions to bulk graphite-like quadratic dispersion behavior is determined by interlayer coupling strength and can be precisely identified by measuring the magnetic field dependence of inter-Landau level transition energies as a function of layer number, predicting a critical layer count beyond which the √B scaling of transition energies systematically breaks down in favor of linear-B dependence characteristic of conventional massive fermions.

abstract: The thermodynamics of nucleic acid processes is heavily affected by the electric double-layer of micro-ions around the polyions. We focus here on the Coulombic contribution to the salt-polyelectrolyte preferential interaction (Donnan) coefficient and we report extremely accurate analytical expressions valid in the range of low salt concentration (when polyion radius is smaller than the Debye length). The analysis is performed at Poisson-Boltzmann level, in cylindrical geometry, with emphasis on highly charged poly-ions (beyond ``counter-ion condensation''). The results hold for any electrolyte of the form $z_-$:$z_+$. We also obtain a remarkably accurate expression for the electric potential in the vicinity of the poly-ion.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the analytical Poisson-Boltzmann expressions derived for the Coulombic preferential interaction coefficients in the low-salt cylindrical geometry regime can be systematically extended to mixed-valence electrolyte systems containing competing mono- and multivalent cations, revealing non-additive ion-specific contributions to counterion condensation that would manifest as measurable deviations from linear superposition predictions in nucleic acid thermodynamic stability assays conducted across a range of mixed-salt concentrations.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the analytical Poisson-Boltzmann expressions derived for the Coulombic preferential interaction coefficients in the low-salt cylindrical geometry regime can be systematically extended to mixed-valence electrolyte systems containing competing mono- and multivalent cations, revealing non-additive ion-specific contributions to counterion condensation that would manifest as measurable deviations from linear superposition predictions in nucleic acid thermodynamic stability assays conducted across a range of mixed-salt concentrations.

abstract: We analyze 27 house price indexes of Las Vegas from Jun. 1983 to Mar. 2005, corresponding to 27 different zip codes. These analyses confirm the existence of a real-estate bubble, defined as a price acceleration faster than exponential, which is found however to be confined to a rather limited time interval in the recent past from approximately 2003 to mid-2004 and has progressively transformed into a more normal growth rate comparable to pre-bubble levels in 2005. There has been no bubble till 2002 except for a medium-sized surge in 1990. In addition, we have identified a strong yearly periodicity which provides a good potential for fine-tuned prediction from month to month. A monthly monitoring using a model that we have developed could confirm, by testing the intra-year structure, if indeed the market has returned to ``normal'' or if more turbulence is expected ahead. We predict the evolution of the indexes one year ahead, which is validated with new data up to Sep. 2006. The present analysis demonstrates the existence of very significant variations at the local scale, in the sense that the bubble in Las Vegas seems to have preceded the more global USA bubble and has ended approximately two years earlier (mid 2004 for Las Vegas compared with mid-2006 for the whole of the USA).
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the early emergence and earlier deflation of the Las Vegas real estate bubble relative to the national U.S. trend reflects a measurable "bubble propagation velocity" driven by speculative investor migration patterns, such that zip codes with higher proportions of non-owner-occupied properties within Las Vegas will exhibit systematically earlier bubble onset dates and steeper super-exponential price acceleration phases compared to predominantly owner-occupied zip codes, a relationship that can be empirically tested by correlating the zip-code-level bubble timing parameters extracted from the log-periodic power law model with contemporaneous investor activity data across the 27 analyzed districts.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the early emergence and earlier deflation of the Las Vegas real estate bubble relative to the national U.S. trend reflects a measurable "bubble propagation velocity" driven by speculative investor migration patterns, such that zip codes with higher proportions of non-owner-occupied properties within Las Vegas will exhibit systematically earlier bubble onset dates and steeper super-exponential price acceleration phases compared to predominantly owner-occupied zip codes, a relationship that can be empirically tested by correlating the zip-code-level bubble timing parameters extracted from the log-periodic power law model with contemporaneous investor activity data across the 27 analyzed districts.

abstract: A quantum critical point (QCP) between the antiferromagnetic and the paramagnetic phases was realized by applying a hydrostatic pressure of ~ 7 GPa on single crystals of NiS_{2} pyrite with a low residual resistivity, rho_{0}, of 0.5 mu-Omega-cm. We found that the critical behavior of the resistivity, rho, in this clean system contrasts sharply with those observed in its disordered analogue, NiS_{2-x}Se_{x} solid-solution, demonstrating the unexpectedly drastic effect of disorder on the quantum criticality. Over a whole paramagnetic region investigated up to P = 9 GPa, a crossover temperature, defined as the onset of T^{2} dependence of rho, an indication of Fermi liquid, was suppressed to a substantially low temperature T sim 2 K and, instead, a non Fermi liquid behavior of rho, T^{3/2}-dependence, robustly showed up.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the T^{3/2} non-Fermi liquid resistivity behavior observed in clean NiS₂ under pressure reflects three-dimensional antiferromagnetic quantum critical fluctuations, and that introducing controlled, low-level Se substitution (x < 0.1) will induce a crossover to a distinct T^{3/2→1} power-law dependence at intermediate temperatures, thereby establishing a quantitative disorder threshold above which impurity scattering fundamentally alters the universality class of the quantum critical point from a clean three-dimensional spin-density-wave type to a disorder-dominated regime.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the T^{3/2} non-Fermi liquid resistivity behavior observed in clean NiS₂ under pressure reflects three-dimensional antiferromagnetic quantum critical fluctuations, and that introducing controlled, low-level Se substitution (x < 0.1) will induce a crossover to a distinct T^{3/2→1} power-law dependence at intermediate temperatures, thereby establishing a quantitative disorder threshold above which impurity scattering fundamentally alters the universality class of the quantum critical point from a clean three-dimensional spin-density-wave type to a disorder-dominated regime.

abstract: The carrier spin coherence in a p-doped GaAs/(Al,Ga)As quantum well with a diluted hole gas has been studied by picosecond pump-probe Kerr rotation with an in-plane magnetic field. For resonant optical excitation of the positively charged exciton the spin precession shows two types of oscillations. Fast oscillating electron spin beats decay with the radiative lifetime of the charged exciton of 50 ps. Long lived spin coherence of the holes with dephasing times up to 650 ps. The spin dephasing time as well as the in-plane hole g factor show strong temperature dependence, underlining the importance of hole localization at cryogenic temperatures.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the long-lived hole spin coherence observed in p-doped GaAs/(Al,Ga)As quantum wells can be further extended beyond 650 ps by introducing intentional spatial modulation of the quantum well thickness, which would enhance hole localization through strain-induced potential fluctuations and thereby reduce the phonon-mediated spin dephasing mechanisms that drive the observed temperature dependence of the hole spin dephasing time.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the long-lived hole spin coherence observed in p-doped GaAs/(Al,Ga)As quantum wells can be further extended beyond 650 ps by introducing intentional spatial modulation of the quantum well thickness, which would enhance hole localization through strain-induced potential fluctuations and thereby reduce the phonon-mediated spin dephasing mechanisms that drive the observed temperature dependence of the hole spin dephasing time.

abstract: We give a microscopic derivation of the Clausius-Mossotti relations for a homogeneous and isotropic magneto-dielectric medium consisting of radiatively broadened atomic oscillators. To this end the diagram series of electromagnetic propagators is calculated exactly for an infinite bi-cubic lattice of dielectric and magnetic dipoles for a lattice constant small compared to the resonance wavelength $\lambda$. Modifications of transition frequencies and linewidth of the elementary oscillators are taken into account in a selfconsistent way by a proper incorporation of the singular self-interaction terms. We show that in radiatively broadened media sufficiently close to the free-space resonance the real part of the index of refraction approaches the value -2 in the limit of $\rho \lambda^3 \gg 1$, where $\rho$ is the number density of scatterers. Since at the same time the imaginary part vanishes as $1/\rho$ local field effects can have important consequences for realizing low-loss negative index materials.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that in a bi-cubic lattice of radiatively broadened magneto-dielectric oscillators with anisotropic spatial arrangements (e.g., tetragonal or orthorhombic symmetry), the local field corrections to the Clausius-Mossotti relations will produce direction-dependent modifications to both the real and imaginary parts of the refractive index, enabling anisotropic negative-index behavior at densities below the isotropic threshold of ρλ³ ≫ 1 and thereby providing a geometric route to reducing absorption losses in negative-index materials without requiring increased scatterer density.
raw_output: We hypothesize that in a bi-cubic lattice of radiatively broadened magneto-dielectric oscillators with anisotropic spatial arrangements (e.g., tetragonal or orthorhombic symmetry), the local field corrections to the Clausius-Mossotti relations will produce direction-dependent modifications to both the real and imaginary parts of the refractive index, enabling anisotropic negative-index behavior at densities below the isotropic threshold of ρλ³ ≫ 1 and thereby providing a geometric route to reducing absorption losses in negative-index materials without requiring increased scatterer density.

abstract: Despite their claimed biological plausibility, most self organizing networks have strict topological constraints and consequently they cannot take into account a wide range of external stimuli. Furthermore their evolution is conditioned by deterministic laws which often are not correlated with the structural parameters and the global status of the network, as it should happen in a real biological system. In nature the environmental inputs are noise affected and fuzzy. Which thing sets the problem to investigate the possibility of emergent behaviour in a not strictly constrained net and subjected to different inputs. It is here presented a new model of Evolutionary Neural Gas (ENG) with any topological constraints, trained by probabilistic laws depending on the local distortion errors and the network dimension. The network is considered as a population of nodes that coexist in an ecosystem sharing local and global resources. Those particular features allow the network to quickly adapt to the environment, according to its dimensions. The ENG model analysis shows that the net evolves as a scale-free graph, and justifies in a deeply physical sense- the term gas here used.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the scale-free topology emerging in the Evolutionary Neural Gas model will exhibit a critical phase transition in its adaptive capacity when environmental noise exceeds a specific threshold relative to network dimension, such that networks operating near this critical point will demonstrate superior generalization performance compared to both subcritical and supercritical noise regimes, as measured by classification accuracy on high-dimensional fuzzy input datasets.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the scale-free topology emerging in the Evolutionary Neural Gas model will exhibit a critical phase transition in its adaptive capacity when environmental noise exceeds a specific threshold relative to network dimension, such that networks operating near this critical point will demonstrate superior generalization performance compared to both subcritical and supercritical noise regimes, as measured by classification accuracy on high-dimensional fuzzy input datasets.

abstract: A recent polarized x-ray absorption experiment on the high temperature cuprate superconductor Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8 indicates the presence of broken parity symmetry below the temperature, T*, where a pseudogap appears in photoemission. We critically analyze the x-ray data, and conclude that a parity-breaking signal of the kind suggested is unlikely based on the crystal structures reported in the literature. Possible other origins of the observed dichroism signal are discussed. We propose x-ray scattering experiments that can be done in order to determine whether such alternative interpretations are valid or not.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dichroism signal observed in polarized x-ray absorption experiments on Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8 originates from structural supermodulation-induced local symmetry breaking rather than intrinsic parity violation, and that this can be definitively distinguished by performing site-specific polarized x-ray absorption measurements at systematically varied momentum transfer vectors corresponding to the known incommensurate supermodulation wavevector, where genuine parity-breaking order would produce a temperature-dependent signal onset at T* while supermodulation artifacts would remain temperature-independent.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the dichroism signal observed in polarized x-ray absorption experiments on Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8 originates from structural supermodulation-induced local symmetry breaking rather than intrinsic parity violation, and that this can be definitively distinguished by performing site-specific polarized x-ray absorption measurements at systematically varied momentum transfer vectors corresponding to the known incommensurate supermodulation wavevector, where genuine parity-breaking order would produce a temperature-dependent signal onset at T* while supermodulation artifacts would remain temperature-independent.

abstract: We present the results of a muon-spin relaxation study of the high-Tc analogue material Cs2AgF4. We find unambiguous evidence for magnetic order, intrinsic to the material, below T_C=13.95(3) K. The ratio of inter- to intraplane coupling is estimated to be |J'/J|=1.9 x 10^-2, while fits of the temperature dependence of the order parameter reveal a critical exponent beta=0.292(3), implying an intermediate character between pure two- and three- dimensional magnetism in the critical regime. Above T_C we observe a signal characteristic of dipolar interactions due to linear F-mu-F bonds, allowing the muon stopping sites in this compound to be characterized.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that partial substitution of Ag with a non-magnetic isoelectronic dopant (such as Pd²⁺) in Cs₂AgF₄ will systematically tune the inter- to intraplane coupling ratio |J'/J| and drive a crossover in the critical exponent β from its intermediate value of ~0.292 toward the purely two-dimensional Ising or XY universality class limits, thereby providing a controlled experimental demonstration of dimensional crossover in a fluoride-based high-Tc analogue system.
raw_output: We hypothesize that partial substitution of Ag with a non-magnetic isoelectronic dopant (such as Pd²⁺) in Cs₂AgF₄ will systematically tune the inter- to intraplane coupling ratio |J'/J| and drive a crossover in the critical exponent β from its intermediate value of ~0.292 toward the purely two-dimensional Ising or XY universality class limits, thereby providing a controlled experimental demonstration of dimensional crossover in a fluoride-based high-Tc analogue system.

abstract: We show that plasmons in two-dimensional graphene can have net gain at terahertz frequencies. The coupling of the plasmons to interband electron-hole transitions in population inverted graphene layers can lead to plasmon amplification through the process of stimulated emission. We calculate plasmon gain for different electron-hole densities and temperatures and show that the gain values can exceed $10^{4}$ cm$^{-1}$ in the 1-10 terahertz frequency range, for electron-hole densities in the $10^{9}$-$10^{11}$ cm$^{-2}$ range, even when plasmon energy loss due to intraband scattering is considered. Plasmons are found to exhibit net gain for intraband scattering times shorter than 100 fs. Such high gain values could allow extremely compact terahertz amplifiers and oscillators that have dimensions in the 1-10 $\mu$m range.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that stacking multiple population-inverted graphene layers separated by thin dielectric spacers will produce a superlinear enhancement of net plasmon gain at terahertz frequencies due to coherent interlayer coupling of stimulated emission processes, enabling oscillation thresholds to be achieved at significantly lower electron-hole densities than predicted for isolated monolayer systems.
raw_output: We hypothesize that stacking multiple population-inverted graphene layers separated by thin dielectric spacers will produce a superlinear enhancement of net plasmon gain at terahertz frequencies due to coherent interlayer coupling of stimulated emission processes, enabling oscillation thresholds to be achieved at significantly lower electron-hole densities than predicted for isolated monolayer systems.

abstract: We investigate the formation of collisionless shocks along the spatial profile of a gaussian laser beam propagating in nonlocal nonlinear media. For defocusing nonlinearity the shock survives the smoothing effect of the nonlocal response, though its dynamics is qualitatively affected by the latter, whereas for focusing nonlinearity it dominates over filamentation. The patterns observed in a thermal defocusing medium are interpreted in the framework of our theory.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that in a medium exhibiting competing focusing and defocusing nonlocal nonlinear responses at different length scales, the spatial profile of collisionless shock formation will undergo a critical transition characterized by a distinct threshold ratio of focusing-to-defocusing nonlocality lengths, below which shock structures are stabilized and above which filamentation instability is restored, producing a measurable bifurcation in the shock front topology that can be experimentally detected via far-field diffraction patterns.
raw_output: We hypothesize that in a medium exhibiting competing focusing and defocusing nonlocal nonlinear responses at different length scales, the spatial profile of collisionless shock formation will undergo a critical transition characterized by a distinct threshold ratio of focusing-to-defocusing nonlocality lengths, below which shock structures are stabilized and above which filamentation instability is restored, producing a measurable bifurcation in the shock front topology that can be experimentally detected via far-field diffraction patterns.

abstract: Efficient control of a laser welding process requires the reliable prediction of process behavior. A statistical method of field modeling, based on normalized RBFNN, can be successfully used to predict the spatiotemporal dynamics of surface optical activity in the laser welding process. In this article we demonstrate how to optimize RBFNN to maximize prediction quality. Special attention is paid to the structure of sample vectors, which represent the bridge between the field distributions in the past and future.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating multi-modal sensor fusion—combining spatiotemporal optical emission data with acoustic and thermal field measurements as composite input vectors—into the normalized RBFNN framework will significantly improve prediction accuracy of subsurface defect formation dynamics in laser welding compared to optical data alone, due to the complementary physical information captured by each modality about keyhole instability and melt pool behavior.
raw_output: We hypothesize that incorporating multi-modal sensor fusion—combining spatiotemporal optical emission data with acoustic and thermal field measurements as composite input vectors—into the normalized RBFNN framework will significantly improve prediction accuracy of subsurface defect formation dynamics in laser welding compared to optical data alone, due to the complementary physical information captured by each modality about keyhole instability and melt pool behavior.

abstract: Transport in metals with strongly anisotropic single-particle spectrum is studied. Coherent band transport in all directions, described by the standard Boltzmann equation, is shown to withstand both elastic and inelastic scattering as long as $E_F\tau\gg 1$. A model of phonon-assisted tunneling via resonant states located in between the layers is suggested to explain a non-monotonic temperature dependence of the c-axis resistivity observed in experiments.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that in quasi-two-dimensional layered metals where interlayer transport occurs via phonon-assisted tunneling through resonant states, a crossover from coherent band transport to incoherent hopping conduction along the c-axis can be induced by systematically reducing the interlayer resonant state density through controlled chemical substitution, which would manifest as a transition from non-monotonic to monotonically increasing c-axis resistivity with decreasing temperature, providing a direct experimental test of the phonon-assisted tunneling mechanism.
raw_output: We hypothesize that in quasi-two-dimensional layered metals where interlayer transport occurs via phonon-assisted tunneling through resonant states, a crossover from coherent band transport to incoherent hopping conduction along the c-axis can be induced by systematically reducing the interlayer resonant state density through controlled chemical substitution, which would manifest as a transition from non-monotonic to monotonically increasing c-axis resistivity with decreasing temperature, providing a direct experimental test of the phonon-assisted tunneling mechanism.

abstract: The parsimony score of a character on a tree equals the number of state changes required to fit that character onto the tree. We show that for unordered, reversible characters this score equals the number of tree rearrangements required to fit the tree onto the character. We discuss implications of this connection for the debate over the use of consensus trees or total evidence, and show how it provides a link between incongruence of characters and recombination.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the mathematical equivalence between parsimony scores and tree rearrangement distances can be extended to ordered (Wagner) characters, such that the weighted parsimony score under a linear state-change cost model will correspond to a specific class of weighted tree rearrangement operations, thereby providing a quantitative framework for detecting recombination breakpoints in genomic regions with stepwise mutational processes.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the mathematical equivalence between parsimony scores and tree rearrangement distances can be extended to ordered (Wagner) characters, such that the weighted parsimony score under a linear state-change cost model will correspond to a specific class of weighted tree rearrangement operations, thereby providing a quantitative framework for detecting recombination breakpoints in genomic regions with stepwise mutational processes.

abstract: In this paper I describe a specialized algorithm for anisotropic diffusion determined by a field of transition rates. The algorithm can be used to describe some interesting forms of diffusion that occur in the study of proton motion in a network of hydrogen bonds. The algorithm produces data that require a nonstandard method of spectral analysis which is also developed here. Finally, I apply the algorithm to a simple specific example.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the anisotropic diffusion algorithm parameterized by hydrogen-bond network transition rates will reveal a non-monotonic dependence of proton conductivity on network topology connectivity, such that intermediate-density hydrogen-bond networks exhibit anomalously enhanced proton transport compared to both sparse and fully connected networks due to resonance-like coupling between directionally correlated diffusion pathways.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the anisotropic diffusion algorithm parameterized by hydrogen-bond network transition rates will reveal a non-monotonic dependence of proton conductivity on network topology connectivity, such that intermediate-density hydrogen-bond networks exhibit anomalously enhanced proton transport compared to both sparse and fully connected networks due to resonance-like coupling between directionally correlated diffusion pathways.

abstract: The temporal evolution of step-edge fluctuations under electromigration conditions is analysed using a continuum Langevin model. If the electromigration driving force acts in the step up/down direction, and step-edge diffusion is the dominant mass-transport mechanism, we find that significant deviations from the usual $t^{1/4}$ scaling of the terrace-width correlation function occurs for a critical time $\tau$ which is dependent upon the three energy scales in the problem: $k_{B}T$, the step stiffness, $\gamma$, and the bias associated with adatom hopping under the influence of an electromigration force, $\pm \Delta U$. For ($t < \tau$), the correlation function evolves as a superposition of $t^{1/4}$ and $t^{3/4}$ power laws. For $t \ge \tau$ a closed form expression can be derived. This behavior is confirmed by a Monte-Carlo simulation using a discrete model of the step dynamics. It is proposed that the magnitude of the electromigration force acting upon an atom at a step-edge can by estimated by a careful analysis of the statistical properties of step-edge fluctuations on the appropriate time-scale.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that when electromigration force is applied parallel to the step edge rather than in the step up/down direction, the crossover time τ and the associated anomalous power-law exponents will differ fundamentally from those derived for the perpendicular case, producing a distinct superposition of scaling regimes that can be experimentally distinguished through autocorrelation analysis of step-edge fluctuations, thereby enabling directional decomposition of the electromigration force vector acting on adatoms at step edges.
raw_output: We hypothesize that when electromigration force is applied parallel to the step edge rather than in the step up/down direction, the crossover time τ and the associated anomalous power-law exponents will differ fundamentally from those derived for the perpendicular case, producing a distinct superposition of scaling regimes that can be experimentally distinguished through autocorrelation analysis of step-edge fluctuations, thereby enabling directional decomposition of the electromigration force vector acting on adatoms at step edges.

abstract: I examine electron-phonon mediated superconductivity in the intermediate coupling and phonon frequency regime of the quasi-2D Holstein model. I use an extended Migdal-Eliashberg theory which includes vertex corrections and spatial fluctuations. I find a d-wave superconducting state that is unique close to half-filling. The order parameter undergoes a transition to s-wave superconductivity on increasing filling. I explain how the inclusion of both vertex corrections and spatial fluctuations is essential for the prediction of a d-wave order parameter. I then discuss the effects of a large Coulomb pseudopotential on the superconductivity (such as is found in contemporary superconducting materials like the cuprates), which results in the destruction of the s-wave states, while leaving the d-wave states unmodified.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that in the quasi-2D Holstein model, the d-wave superconducting state near half-filling exhibits a characteristic doping-dependent transition temperature dome that peaks at an optimal filling fraction determined by the competition between vertex correction-enhanced pairing and spatial fluctuation-induced pair-breaking, such that this dome would be experimentally distinguishable from conventional s-wave BCS behavior through a non-monotonic response of the superfluid density to controlled introduction of non-magnetic impurities, which would selectively suppress d-wave but not s-wave order.
raw_output: We hypothesize that in the quasi-2D Holstein model, the d-wave superconducting state near half-filling exhibits a characteristic doping-dependent transition temperature dome that peaks at an optimal filling fraction determined by the competition between vertex correction-enhanced pairing and spatial fluctuation-induced pair-breaking, such that this dome would be experimentally distinguishable from conventional s-wave BCS behavior through a non-monotonic response of the superfluid density to controlled introduction of non-magnetic impurities, which would selectively suppress d-wave but not s-wave order.

abstract: A coarse-grained computational procedure based on the Finite Element Method is proposed to calculate the normal modes and mechanical response of proteins and their supramolecular assemblies. Motivated by the elastic network model, proteins are modeled as homogeneous isotropic elastic solids with volume defined by their solvent-excluded surface. The discretized Finite Element representation is obtained using a surface simplification algorithm that facilitates the generation of models of arbitrary prescribed spatial resolution. The procedure is applied to compute the normal modes of a mutant of T4 phage lysozyme and of filamentous actin, as well as the critical Euler buckling load of the latter when subject to axial compression. Results compare favorably with all-atom normal mode analysis, the Rotation Translation Blocks procedure, and experiment. The proposed methodology establishes a computational framework for the calculation of protein mechanical response that facilitates the incorporation of specific atomic-level interactions into the model, including aqueous-electrolyte-mediated electrostatic effects. The procedure is equally applicable to proteins with known atomic coordinates as it is to electron density maps of proteins, protein complexes, and supramolecular assemblies of unknown atomic structure.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating spatially heterogeneous elastic moduli into the finite element model—derived from local packing density or residue-specific force constants obtained from all-atom simulations—will more accurately predict anisotropic mechanical responses and allosteric communication pathways in proteins than the homogeneous isotropic elastic solid assumption, particularly for proteins with structurally distinct rigid and flexible domains.
raw_output: We hypothesize that incorporating spatially heterogeneous elastic moduli into the finite element model—derived from local packing density or residue-specific force constants obtained from all-atom simulations—will more accurately predict anisotropic mechanical responses and allosteric communication pathways in proteins than the homogeneous isotropic elastic solid assumption, particularly for proteins with structurally distinct rigid and flexible domains.

abstract: Two-dimensional correlation spectroscopy (2DCS) based on the nonlinear optical response of excitons to sequences of ultrafast pulses, has the potential to provide some unique insights into carrier dynamics in semiconductors. The most prominent feature of 2DCS, cross peaks, can best be understood using a sum-over-states picture involving the many-body eigenstates. However, the optical response of semiconductors is usually calculated by solving truncated equations of motion for dynamical variables, which result in a quasiparticle picture. In this work we derive Green's function expressions for the four wave mixing signals generated in various phase-matching directions and use them to establish the connection between the two pictures. The formal connection with Frenkel excitons (hard-core bosons) and vibrational excitons (soft-core bosons) is pointed out.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Green's function formalism connecting the sum-over-states and quasiparticle pictures in semiconductor 2DCS can be extended to predict quantitatively distinct cross-peak signatures that differentiate hard-core (Frenkel) from soft-core (vibrational) bosonic exciton statistics, such that the anharmonicity-dependent lineshape asymmetry and cross-peak amplitude ratios in two-dimensional four-wave mixing spectra serve as a direct experimental observable for determining the effective bosonic character of excitons in a given semiconductor system.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the Green's function formalism connecting the sum-over-states and quasiparticle pictures in semiconductor 2DCS can be extended to predict quantitatively distinct cross-peak signatures that differentiate hard-core (Frenkel) from soft-core (vibrational) bosonic exciton statistics, such that the anharmonicity-dependent lineshape asymmetry and cross-peak amplitude ratios in two-dimensional four-wave mixing spectra serve as a direct experimental observable for determining the effective bosonic character of excitons in a given semiconductor system.

abstract: In this paper a theoretical model of functioning of a neural circuit during a behavioral response has been proposed. A neural circuit can be thought of as a directed multigraph whose each vertex is a neuron and each edge is a synapse. It has been assumed in this paper that the behavior of such circuits is manifested through the collective behavior of neurons belonging to that circuit. Behavioral information of each neuron is contained in the coefficients of the fast Fourier transform (FFT) over the output spike train. Those coefficients form a vector in a multidimensional vector space. Behavioral dynamics of a neuronal network in response to strong aversive stimuli has been studied in a vector space in which a suitable pseudometric has been defined. The neurodynamical model of network behavior has been formulated in terms of existing memory, synaptic plasticity and feelings. The model has an analogy in classical electrostatics, by which the notion of force and potential energy has been introduced. Since the model takes input from each neuron in a network and produces a behavior as the output, it would be extremely difficult or may even be impossible to implement. But with the help of the model a possible explanation for an hitherto unexplained neurological observation in human brain has been offered. The model is compatible with a recent model of sequential behavioral dynamics. The model is based on electrophysiology, but its relevance to hemodynamics has been outlined.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the pseudometric-defined vector space distances between FFT coefficient vectors of individual neurons within a circuit will exhibit measurable correlations with BOLD signal amplitudes in fMRI recordings during aversive stimuli, such that neurons whose spike train FFT vectors undergo the greatest displacement in the behavioral dynamics model will correspond to hemodynamically active regions with disproportionately elevated blood-oxygen-level-dependent responses, thereby providing an empirically testable bridge between the electrophysiological and hemodynamic components of the proposed model.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the pseudometric-defined vector space distances between FFT coefficient vectors of individual neurons within a circuit will exhibit measurable correlations with BOLD signal amplitudes in fMRI recordings during aversive stimuli, such that neurons whose spike train FFT vectors undergo the greatest displacement in the behavioral dynamics model will correspond to hemodynamically active regions with disproportionately elevated blood-oxygen-level-dependent responses, thereby providing an empirically testable bridge between the electrophysiological and hemodynamic components of the proposed model.

abstract: We show that the cold atom systems of simultaneously trapped Bose-Einstein condensates (BEC's) and quantum degenerate fermionic atoms provide promising laboratories for the study of macroscopic quantum tunneling. Our theoretical studies reveal that the spatial extent of a small trapped BEC immersed in a Fermi sea can tunnel and coherently oscillate between the values of the separated and mixed configurations (the phases of the phase separation transition of BEC-fermion systems). We evaluate the period, amplitude and dissipation rate for $^{23}$Na and $^{40}$K-atoms and we discuss the experimental prospects for observing this phenomenon.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the coherent oscillation period and dissipation rate of macroscopic quantum tunneling in BEC-fermion mixtures can be systematically controlled by tuning the interspecies scattering length via Feshbach resonance, such that approaching the resonance condition will exponentially suppress the tunneling amplitude by increasing the effective potential barrier between phase-separated and mixed configurations, thereby providing a precise experimental knob for mapping the quantum-to-classical transition in this macroscopic system.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the coherent oscillation period and dissipation rate of macroscopic quantum tunneling in BEC-fermion mixtures can be systematically controlled by tuning the interspecies scattering length via Feshbach resonance, such that approaching the resonance condition will exponentially suppress the tunneling amplitude by increasing the effective potential barrier between phase-separated and mixed configurations, thereby providing a precise experimental knob for mapping the quantum-to-classical transition in this macroscopic system.

abstract: We propose a new concept for the design of high-efficiency photocells based on ultra-thin (submicron) semiconductor films of controlled thickness. Using a microscopic model of a thin dielectric layer interacting with incident electromagnetic radiation we evaluate the efficiency of conversion of solar radiation into the electric power. We determine the optimal range of parameters which maximize the efficiency of such photovoltaic element.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that stacking multiple ultra-thin semiconductor films of sequentially graded bandgap energies, each individually optimized to a specific spectral window of the solar spectrum, will yield a multi-junction photocell architecture whose overall power conversion efficiency surpasses that of any single optimally-tuned submicron layer by exploiting complementary photon absorption across discrete wavelength bands while maintaining the submicron total thickness regime.
raw_output: We hypothesize that stacking multiple ultra-thin semiconductor films of sequentially graded bandgap energies, each individually optimized to a specific spectral window of the solar spectrum, will yield a multi-junction photocell architecture whose overall power conversion efficiency surpasses that of any single optimally-tuned submicron layer by exploiting complementary photon absorption across discrete wavelength bands while maintaining the submicron total thickness regime.

abstract: We report on the realization of a time-domain `St\"uckelberg interferometer', which is based on the internal state structure of ultracold Feshbach molecules. Two subsequent passages through a weak avoided crossing between two different orbital angular momentum states in combination with a variable hold time lead to high-contrast population oscillations. This allows for a precise determination of the energy difference between the two molecular states. We demonstrate a high degree of control over the interferometer dynamics. The interferometric scheme provides new possibilities for precision measurements with ultracold molecules.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that by introducing a controlled external perturbation (such as a precisely calibrated magnetic field gradient or optical lattice potential) during the hold time phase of the Stückelberg interferometer, it will be possible to detect and quantify weak intermolecular interaction forces between ultracold Feshbach molecules with sensitivity exceeding that of conventional spectroscopic methods, enabling the measurement of previously unresolved anisotropic interaction potentials between different orbital angular momentum states.
raw_output: We hypothesize that by introducing a controlled external perturbation (such as a precisely calibrated magnetic field gradient or optical lattice potential) during the hold time phase of the Stückelberg interferometer, it will be possible to detect and quantify weak intermolecular interaction forces between ultracold Feshbach molecules with sensitivity exceeding that of conventional spectroscopic methods, enabling the measurement of previously unresolved anisotropic interaction potentials between different orbital angular momentum states.

abstract: A theory of collective states in a magnetically quantized two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG) with half-filled Landau level (quantized Hall ferromagnet) in the presence of magnetic 3d impurities is developed. The spectrum of bound and delocalized spin-excitons as well as the renormalization of Zeeman splitting of the impurity 3d levels due to the indirect exchange interaction with the 2DEG are studied for the specific case of n-type GaAs doped with Mn where the Lande` g-factors of impurity and 2DEG have opposite signs. If the sign of the 2DEG g-factor is changed due to external influences, then impurity related transitions to new ground state phases, presenting various spin-flip and skyrmion-like textures, are possible. Conditions for existence of these phases are discussed. PACS: 73.43.Lp, 73.21.Fg, 72.15.Rn
hypothesis: We hypothesize that in n-type GaAs doped with Mn, the application of hydrostatic pressure—which continuously tunes the 2DEG g-factor through zero by modifying the band structure—will induce a sequence of distinct quantum phase transitions between spin-flip and skyrmion-like ground state textures at critical pressure values that are predictable from the renormalized impurity Zeeman splitting, thereby providing a direct experimental probe of the indirect exchange coupling strength between the magnetic 3d impurities and the half-filled Landau level.
raw_output: We hypothesize that in n-type GaAs doped with Mn, the application of hydrostatic pressure—which continuously tunes the 2DEG g-factor through zero by modifying the band structure—will induce a sequence of distinct quantum phase transitions between spin-flip and skyrmion-like ground state textures at critical pressure values that are predictable from the renormalized impurity Zeeman splitting, thereby providing a direct experimental probe of the indirect exchange coupling strength between the magnetic 3d impurities and the half-filled Landau level.

abstract: When the DNA double helix is subjected to external forces it can stretch elastically to elongations reaching 100% of its natural length. These distortions, imposed at the mesoscopic or macroscopic scales, have a dramatic effect on electronic properties at the atomic scale and on electrical transport along DNA. Accordingly, a multiscale approach is necessary to capture the electronic behavior of the stretched DNA helix. To construct such a model, we begin with accurate density-functional-theory calculations for electronic states in DNA bases and base pairs in various relative configurations encountered in the equilibrium and stretched forms. These results are complemented by semi-empirical quantum mechanical calculations for the states of a small size [18 base pair poly(CG)-poly(CG)] dry, neutral DNA sequence, using previously published models for stretched DNA. The calculated electronic states are then used to parametrize an effective tight-binding model that can describe electron hopping in the presence of environmental effects, such as the presence of stray water molecules on the backbone or structural features of the substrate. These effects introduce disorder in the model hamiltonian which leads to electron localization. The localization length is smaller by several orders of magnitude in stretched DNA relative to that in the unstretched structure.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dramatic reduction in electron localization length observed in mechanically stretched DNA can be exploited as a sequence-dependent mechanical biosensing mechanism, whereby specific nucleotide sequences exhibiting distinct electronic localization responses to controlled elongation forces can be identified through characteristic changes in electrical conductance, enabling single-molecule DNA sequencing without the need for fluorescent labeling or chemical modification.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the dramatic reduction in electron localization length observed in mechanically stretched DNA can be exploited as a sequence-dependent mechanical biosensing mechanism, whereby specific nucleotide sequences exhibiting distinct electronic localization responses to controlled elongation forces can be identified through characteristic changes in electrical conductance, enabling single-molecule DNA sequencing without the need for fluorescent labeling or chemical modification.

abstract: We show that recent stock market fluctuations are characterized by the cumulative distributions whose tails on short, minute time scales exhibit power scaling with the scaling index alpha > 3 and this index tends to increase quickly with decreasing sampling frequency. Our study is based on high-frequency recordings of the S&P500, DAX and WIG20 indices over the interval May 2004 - May 2006. Our findings suggest that dynamics of the contemporary market may differ from the one observed in the past. This effect indicates a constantly increasing efficiency of world markets.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the observed temporal increase in the power-law scaling index alpha of stock market return distributions reflects a quantifiable relationship with market microstructure variables—specifically trading volume, bid-ask spreads, and algorithmic trading participation rates—such that markets with higher proportions of high-frequency algorithmic trading will exhibit significantly faster convergence of tail distributions toward Gaussian behavior across sampling frequencies compared to markets dominated by human traders.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the observed temporal increase in the power-law scaling index alpha of stock market return distributions reflects a quantifiable relationship with market microstructure variables—specifically trading volume, bid-ask spreads, and algorithmic trading participation rates—such that markets with higher proportions of high-frequency algorithmic trading will exhibit significantly faster convergence of tail distributions toward Gaussian behavior across sampling frequencies compared to markets dominated by human traders.

abstract: The problem of statistical learning is to construct a predictor of a random variable $Y$ as a function of a related random variable $X$ on the basis of an i.i.d. training sample from the joint distribution of $(X,Y)$. Allowable predictors are drawn from some specified class, and the goal is to approach asymptotically the performance (expected loss) of the best predictor in the class. We consider the setting in which one has perfect observation of the $X$-part of the sample, while the $Y$-part has to be communicated at some finite bit rate. The encoding of the $Y$-values is allowed to depend on the $X$-values. Under suitable regularity conditions on the admissible predictors, the underlying family of probability distributions and the loss function, we give an information-theoretic characterization of achievable predictor performance in terms of conditional distortion-rate functions. The ideas are illustrated on the example of nonparametric regression in Gaussian noise.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that when the bit-rate constraint on the Y-observations is allowed to vary adaptively as a function of the local complexity of the predictor class—rather than being fixed globally—the resulting achievable predictor performance can be characterized by a localized conditional distortion-rate function that yields strictly tighter excess risk bounds than those obtained under uniform rate allocation, particularly in heteroscedastic nonparametric regression settings.
raw_output: We hypothesize that when the bit-rate constraint on the Y-observations is allowed to vary adaptively as a function of the local complexity of the predictor class—rather than being fixed globally—the resulting achievable predictor performance can be characterized by a localized conditional distortion-rate function that yields strictly tighter excess risk bounds than those obtained under uniform rate allocation, particularly in heteroscedastic nonparametric regression settings.

abstract: Suprathreshold stochastic resonance (SSR) is a form of noise enhanced signal transmission that occurs in a parallel array of independently noisy identical threshold nonlinearities, including model neurons. Unlike most forms of stochastic resonance, the output response to suprathreshold random input signals of arbitrary magnitude is improved by the presence of even small amounts of noise. In this paper the information transmission performance of SSR in the limit of a large array size is considered. Using a relationship between Shannon's mutual information and Fisher information, a sufficient condition for optimality, i.e. channel capacity, is derived. It is shown that capacity is achieved when the signal distribution is Jeffrey's prior, as formed from the noise distribution, or when the noise distribution depends on the signal distribution via a cosine relationship. These results provide theoretical verification and justification for previous work in both computational neuroscience and electronics.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that in biological neural populations where the endogenous noise distribution approximates a cosine relationship with the natural stimulus distribution, the system will exhibit channel capacity-approaching information transmission efficiency, and that deviations from this cosine relationship—measurable via Fisher information analysis of neural spike trains—will predict degraded sensory discrimination performance in psychophysical tasks.
raw_output: We hypothesize that in biological neural populations where the endogenous noise distribution approximates a cosine relationship with the natural stimulus distribution, the system will exhibit channel capacity-approaching information transmission efficiency, and that deviations from this cosine relationship—measurable via Fisher information analysis of neural spike trains—will predict degraded sensory discrimination performance in psychophysical tasks.

abstract: We report on a new type of experiment that enables us to monitor spatially and temporally heterogeneous dynamic properties in complex fluids. Our approach is based on the analysis of near-field speckles produced by light diffusely reflected from the superficial volume of a strongly scattering medium. By periodic modulation of an incident speckle beam we obtain pixel-wise ensemble averages of the structure function coefficient, a measure of the dynamic activity. To illustrate the application of our approach we follow the different stages in the drying process of a colloidal thin film. We show that we can access ensemble averaged dynamic properties on length scales as small as ten micrometers over the full field of view.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the near-field speckle analysis technique described can detect spatially heterogeneous gelation dynamics in biopolymer networks (such as collagen or fibrin) at the microscale, revealing that local sol-gel transition kinetics vary systematically with distance from nucleation sites in a manner that bulk rheological measurements cannot resolve.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the near-field speckle analysis technique described can detect spatially heterogeneous gelation dynamics in biopolymer networks (such as collagen or fibrin) at the microscale, revealing that local sol-gel transition kinetics vary systematically with distance from nucleation sites in a manner that bulk rheological measurements cannot resolve.
