In the two-dimensional case at $\beta e^2=2$, we have now explicit expressions up to order $\hbar^2$ of these two quantum densities, thus we can microscopically check the sum rules at this order. The checks are positive, reinforcing the idea that the sum rules are correct.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Wigner-Kirkwood quantum corrections to order ℏ⁴ for the two-dimensional one-component plasma at βe²=2 in a half-plane geometry will continue to satisfy the macroscopic sum rules governing the asymptotic truncated two-body density and the dipole moment of the structure factor, and that this order-by-order consistency can be formally proven by demonstrating that the sum rules are protected by a Ward identity associated with gauge invariance of the underlying quantum Coulomb system.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the Wigner-Kirkwood quantum corrections to order ℏ⁴ for the two-dimensional one-component plasma at βe²=2 in a half-plane geometry will continue to satisfy the macroscopic sum rules governing the asymptotic truncated two-body density and the dipole moment of the structure factor, and that this order-by-order consistency can be formally proven by demonstrating that the sum rules are protected by a Ward identity associated with gauge invariance of the underlying quantum Coulomb system.

abstract: The Method of Invariant Grid (MIG) is an iterative procedure for model reduction in chemical kinetics which is based on the notion of Slow Invariant Manifold (SIM) [1-4]. Important role, in that method, is played by the initial grid which, once refined, gives a description of the invariant manifold: the invariant grid. A convenient way to get a first approximation of the SIM is given by the Spectral Quasi Equilibrium Manifold (SQEM) [1-2]. In the present paper, a flexible numerical method to construct the discrete analog of a Quasi Equilibrium Manifold, in any dimension, is presented. That object is named Quasi Equilibrium Grid (QEG), while the procedure Quasi Equilibrium Grid Algorithm. Extensions of the QEM notion are also suggested. The QEG is a numerical tool which can be used to find a grid-based approximation for the locus of minima of a convex function under some linear constraints. The method is validated by construction of one and two-dimensional grids for model hydrogen oxidation reaction.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Quasi Equilibrium Grid Algorithm, when extended to incorporate adaptive mesh refinement strategies that concentrate grid points in regions of high curvature of the slow invariant manifold, will yield significantly faster convergence to the true invariant manifold during Method of Invariant Grid iterations compared to uniform grid initializations, particularly in multi-dimensional chemical kinetic systems exhibiting strongly anisotropic relaxation timescales.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the Quasi Equilibrium Grid Algorithm, when extended to incorporate adaptive mesh refinement strategies that concentrate grid points in regions of high curvature of the slow invariant manifold, will yield significantly faster convergence to the true invariant manifold during Method of Invariant Grid iterations compared to uniform grid initializations, particularly in multi-dimensional chemical kinetic systems exhibiting strongly anisotropic relaxation timescales.

abstract: As an alternative to commonly used electrical methods, we have investigated the optical pumping of charged exciton complexes addressing impurity related transitions with photons of the appropriate energy. Under these conditions, we demonstrate that the pumping fidelity can be very high while still maintaining a switching behavior between the different excitonic species. This mechanism has been investigated for single quantum dots of different size present in the same sample and compared with the direct injection of spectator electrons from nearby donors.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the optical pumping fidelity of charged exciton complexes in single quantum dots can be further enhanced by exploiting the polarization state of the pump photons to selectively address spin-resolved impurity transitions, thereby enabling deterministic spin-state initialization with fidelity exceeding that achievable through unpolarized optical pumping or direct spectator electron injection from nearby donors.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the optical pumping fidelity of charged exciton complexes in single quantum dots can be further enhanced by exploiting the polarization state of the pump photons to selectively address spin-resolved impurity transitions, thereby enabling deterministic spin-state initialization with fidelity exceeding that achievable through unpolarized optical pumping or direct spectator electron injection from nearby donors.

abstract: A consistent device model to describe current-voltage characteristics of metal/insulator/metal systems is developed. In this model the insulator and the metal electrodes are described within the same theoretical framework by using density of states distributions. This approach leads to differential equations for the electric field which have to be solved in a self consistent manner by considering the continuity of the electric displacement and the electrochemical potential in the complete system. The model is capable of describing the current-voltage characteristics of the metal/insulator/metal system in forward and reverse bias for arbitrary values of the metal/ insulator injection barriers. In the case of high injection barriers, approximations are provided offering a tool for comparison with experiments. Numerical calculations are performed exemplary using a simplified model of an organic semiconductor.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating spatially inhomogeneous density of states distributions within the insulator layer of the metal/insulator/metal model—arising from structural defects, grain boundaries, or intentional dopant gradients—will produce asymmetric current-voltage characteristics that cannot be captured by uniform-DOS approximations, and that this asymmetry can be quantitatively predicted by extending the self-consistent electrochemical potential framework to account for position-dependent trap state energies and densities.
raw_output: We hypothesize that incorporating spatially inhomogeneous density of states distributions within the insulator layer of the metal/insulator/metal model—arising from structural defects, grain boundaries, or intentional dopant gradients—will produce asymmetric current-voltage characteristics that cannot be captured by uniform-DOS approximations, and that this asymmetry can be quantitatively predicted by extending the self-consistent electrochemical potential framework to account for position-dependent trap state energies and densities.

abstract: The bandstructure was calculated by the full-potential linearized augmented plane wave method. The result reveals two important insights to the novel second harmonic generation (SHG) of alpha-phase lithium iodate ($\alpha-LiIO_{3}$) crystal: the existence of finite intra-band momentum matrix elements due to the non-inversion symmetry of the crystal illuminating the potential of the intra-band transition, and the strong covalent bonding between the $I$-atoms and the ligand $O$-atoms resulting the condition of the double-resonance. An inter-band transition scenario in SHG as $\alpha-LiIO_{3}$ in nano-structure is proposed. The optical properties were calculated within the theoretical framework of the time-dependent perturbation of the independent-particle model. The dielectric tensors and the refractive index were evaluated. Comparisons between the predictions and the results were made: the x-ray near edge absorption spectra; the refractive index at the static limit, and at finite frequencies. Possible factors attributing the calculation errors is discussed.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that reducing the dimensionality of α-LiIO₃ to a nanostructured form (e.g., quantum dots or nanowires below a critical size threshold) will enhance the double-resonance condition arising from I–O covalent bonding by quantum confinement-induced modification of the inter-band transition energies, thereby producing a measurable size-dependent amplification of the second harmonic generation efficiency that scales inversely with nanostructure diameter.
raw_output: We hypothesize that reducing the dimensionality of α-LiIO₃ to a nanostructured form (e.g., quantum dots or nanowires below a critical size threshold) will enhance the double-resonance condition arising from I–O covalent bonding by quantum confinement-induced modification of the inter-band transition energies, thereby producing a measurable size-dependent amplification of the second harmonic generation efficiency that scales inversely with nanostructure diameter.

abstract: We consider a social system of interacting heterogeneous agents with learning abilities, a model close to Random Field Ising Models, where the random field corresponds to the idiosyncratic willingness to pay. Given a fixed price, agents decide repeatedly whether to buy or not a unit of a good, so as to maximize their expected utilities. We show that the equilibrium reached by the system depends on the nature of the information agents use to estimate their expected utilities.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that in heterogeneous agent social learning systems analogous to Random Field Ising Models, the introduction of a small fraction of "stubborn" agents with fixed, non-updating beliefs will induce phase transitions in collective purchasing equilibria at critical density thresholds that are quantitatively predictable from the distribution parameters of the underlying random field, such that even a minority of stubborn agents can shift the entire system from one stable equilibrium to another when their idiosyncratic willingness-to-pay values are strategically positioned near the critical price point.
raw_output: We hypothesize that in heterogeneous agent social learning systems analogous to Random Field Ising Models, the introduction of a small fraction of "stubborn" agents with fixed, non-updating beliefs will induce phase transitions in collective purchasing equilibria at critical density thresholds that are quantitatively predictable from the distribution parameters of the underlying random field, such that even a minority of stubborn agents can shift the entire system from one stable equilibrium to another when their idiosyncratic willingness-to-pay values are strategically positioned near the critical price point.

abstract: The process of annealing of a CdTe:Cl ingot during its cooling after growth was studied. The annealing was performed in two stages: a high-temperature stage, with an approximate equality of chlorine and cadmium vacancy concentrations established at the thermodynamic equilibrium between the crystal and vapors of volatile components, and a low-temperature stage, with charged defects interacting to form neutral associations. The chlorine concentrations necessary to obtain semi-insulating crystals were determined for various ingot cooling rates in the high temperature stage. The dependence of the chlorine concentration [Cl+Te] in the ingot on the temperature of annealing in the high-temperature stage was found. The carrier lifetimes and drift mobilities were obtained in relation to the temperature and cadmium vapor pressure in the postgrowth annealing of the ingot.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the introduction of a controlled intermediate-temperature annealing stage between the high-temperature equilibration stage and the low-temperature defect association stage will preferentially promote the formation of specific neutral Cl-vacancy complexes that enhance carrier lifetime while simultaneously suppressing competing recombination centers, thereby enabling semi-insulating CdTe:Cl crystals with superior drift mobility-lifetime products compared to those achievable through conventional two-stage annealing protocols.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the introduction of a controlled intermediate-temperature annealing stage between the high-temperature equilibration stage and the low-temperature defect association stage will preferentially promote the formation of specific neutral Cl-vacancy complexes that enhance carrier lifetime while simultaneously suppressing competing recombination centers, thereby enabling semi-insulating CdTe:Cl crystals with superior drift mobility-lifetime products compared to those achievable through conventional two-stage annealing protocols.

abstract: We investigate the behavior of the directed current in one-dimensional systems of Dirac fermions driven by local periodic potentials in the forward as well in backscattering channels. We treat the problem with Keldysh non-equilibrium Green's function formalism. We present the exact solution for the case of an infinite wire and show that in this case the dc current vanishes identically. We also investigate a confined system consistent in an annular arrangement coupled to a particle reservoir. We present a perturbative treatment that allows for the analytical expressions of the dc current in the lowest order of the amplitudes of the potential. We also present results obtained from the exact numerical solution of the problem.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that introducing a spatially asymmetric periodic driving potential (breaking left-right symmetry) in the annular Dirac fermion system coupled to a particle reservoir will generate a non-vanishing, tunable directed dc current even at the lowest perturbative order, with the current magnitude and direction controllable by the relative phase between forward and backscattering potential components.
raw_output: We hypothesize that introducing a spatially asymmetric periodic driving potential (breaking left-right symmetry) in the annular Dirac fermion system coupled to a particle reservoir will generate a non-vanishing, tunable directed dc current even at the lowest perturbative order, with the current magnitude and direction controllable by the relative phase between forward and backscattering potential components.

abstract: Processes of growth of semi-insulating Cd(1-x)Zn(x)Te:Cl crystals (x = 0.0002 and 0.1) of n-type conductivity are investigated. From the grown crystals detectors for X-ray computer tomography with small value of photocurrent memory (afterglow) (0.1-0.3%) are obtained.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that increasing the zinc mole fraction (x) beyond 0.1 in Cl-doped Cd(1-x)Zn(x)Te crystals will further reduce afterglow below 0.1% by progressively widening the bandgap and thereby increasing the ionization energy of shallow trap states responsible for delayed charge carrier release, with an optimal zinc concentration existing at which detector sensitivity and afterglow suppression are simultaneously maximized.
raw_output: We hypothesize that increasing the zinc mole fraction (x) beyond 0.1 in Cl-doped Cd(1-x)Zn(x)Te crystals will further reduce afterglow below 0.1% by progressively widening the bandgap and thereby increasing the ionization energy of shallow trap states responsible for delayed charge carrier release, with an optimal zinc concentration existing at which detector sensitivity and afterglow suppression are simultaneously maximized.

abstract: We consider a model of socially interacting individuals that make a binary choice in a context of positive additive endogenous externalities. It encompasses as particular cases several models from the sociology and economics literature. We extend previous results to the case of a general distribution of idiosyncratic preferences, called here Idiosyncratic Willingnesses to Pay (IWP). Positive additive externalities yield a family of inverse demand curves that include the classical downward sloping ones but also new ones with non constant convexity. When j, the ratio of the social influence strength to the standard deviation of the IWP distribution, is small enough, the inverse demand is a classical monotonic (decreasing) function of the adoption rate. Even if the IWP distribution is mono-modal, there is a critical value of j above which the inverse demand is non monotonic, decreasing for small and high adoption rates, but increasing within some intermediate range. Depending on the price there are thus either one or two equilibria. Beyond this first result, we exhibit the generic properties of the boundaries limiting the regions where the system presents different types of equilibria (unique or multiple). These properties are shown to depend only on qualitative features of the IWP distribution: modality (number of maxima), smoothness and type of support (compact or infinite). The main results are summarized as phase diagrams in the space of the model parameters, on which the regions of multiple equilibria are precisely delimited.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that when the idiosyncratic willingness-to-pay distribution is bimodal—reflecting a population polarized into two distinct preference groups—the critical threshold value of j above which non-monotonic inverse demand emerges will be substantially lower than for unimodal distributions, and the resulting phase diagram will exhibit a qualitatively distinct topology featuring additional regions of multiple equilibria that are absent in the unimodal case, a prediction that can be tested by systematically varying the separation between the two modes while holding the social influence strength constant and mapping the resulting equilibrium boundaries.
raw_output: We hypothesize that when the idiosyncratic willingness-to-pay distribution is bimodal—reflecting a population polarized into two distinct preference groups—the critical threshold value of j above which non-monotonic inverse demand emerges will be substantially lower than for unimodal distributions, and the resulting phase diagram will exhibit a qualitatively distinct topology featuring additional regions of multiple equilibria that are absent in the unimodal case, a prediction that can be tested by systematically varying the separation between the two modes while holding the social influence strength constant and mapping the resulting equilibrium boundaries.

abstract: Elastic neutron diffraction measurements were performed on the quasi-two dimensional heavy fermion system CeRh1-xCoxIn5, ranging from an incommensurate antiferromagnet for low x to an unconventional superconductor on the Co-rich end of the phase diagram. We found that the superconductivity competes with the incommensurate antiferromagnetic (AFM) order characterized by qI=(1/2, 1/2, delta) with delta=0.298, while it coexists with the commensurate AFM order with qc=(1/2, 1/2, 1/2). This is in sharp contrast to the CeRh1-xIrxIn5 system, where both the commensurate and incommensurate magnetic orders coexist with the superconductivity. These results reveal that particular areas on the Fermi surface nested by qI play an active role in forming the superconducting state in CeCoIn5.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying hydrostatic pressure to compositions of CeRh₁₋ₓCoxIn₅ near the incommensurate-to-commensurate magnetic order transition boundary will induce a tunable crossover in the superconducting pairing symmetry, detectable via changes in the superconducting gap nodes measured by angle-resolved specific heat or thermal conductivity, because the selective suppression of the incommensurate nesting vector q_I=(1/2, 1/2, 0.298) under pressure will alter the Fermi surface topology that drives unconventional pairing in CeCoIn₅.
raw_output: We hypothesize that applying hydrostatic pressure to compositions of CeRh₁₋ₓCoxIn₅ near the incommensurate-to-commensurate magnetic order transition boundary will induce a tunable crossover in the superconducting pairing symmetry, detectable via changes in the superconducting gap nodes measured by angle-resolved specific heat or thermal conductivity, because the selective suppression of the incommensurate nesting vector q_I=(1/2, 1/2, 0.298) under pressure will alter the Fermi surface topology that drives unconventional pairing in CeCoIn₅.

abstract: We show in spatially one dimensional Madelung fluid that a simple requirement on local stability of the maximum of quantum probability density will, if combined with the global scale invariance of quantum potential, lead to a class of quantum probability densities globally being self-trapped by their own self-generated quantum potentials, possessing only a finite-size spatial support. It turns out to belong to a class of the most probable wave function given its energy through the maximum entropy principle. We proceed to show that there is a limiting case in which the quantum probability density becomes the stationary-moving soliton-like solution of the Schr\"odinger equation.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that in higher-dimensional Madelung fluid systems (two or three spatial dimensions), the combined constraints of local stability at the quantum probability density maximum and global scale invariance of the quantum potential will yield self-trapped probability density solutions with finite spatial support whose geometry (spherical, toroidal, or otherwise) is uniquely determined by the dimensionality of the system, producing distinct classes of soliton-like solutions with dimension-dependent energy-entropy relationships that deviate measurably from their one-dimensional counterparts.
raw_output: We hypothesize that in higher-dimensional Madelung fluid systems (two or three spatial dimensions), the combined constraints of local stability at the quantum probability density maximum and global scale invariance of the quantum potential will yield self-trapped probability density solutions with finite spatial support whose geometry (spherical, toroidal, or otherwise) is uniquely determined by the dimensionality of the system, producing distinct classes of soliton-like solutions with dimension-dependent energy-entropy relationships that deviate measurably from their one-dimensional counterparts.

abstract: We construct a hierarchy of lattice fermions, where the coarser lattice Dirac operator is the Schur complement of the block UL decomposition of the finer lattice operator. We show that the construction is an exact gauged renormalisation group transformation of the lattice action. In particular, using such a transformation and the QCDLAB tool, it is shown how to implement the Ginsparg-Wilson strategy for chiral fermions in the presence of a dynamical gauge field. The scheme allows, for the first time, a full multigrid algorithm for lattice quarks.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Schur complement-based renormalization group hierarchy constructed for lattice fermions can be systematically extended to incorporate dynamical gauge field fluctuations at each coarsening level, such that the resulting multigrid preconditioner exhibits a condition number that scales polynomially rather than exponentially with the inverse quark mass, thereby overcoming critical slowing down near the chiral limit more effectively than conventional iterative solvers.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the Schur complement-based renormalization group hierarchy constructed for lattice fermions can be systematically extended to incorporate dynamical gauge field fluctuations at each coarsening level, such that the resulting multigrid preconditioner exhibits a condition number that scales polynomially rather than exponentially with the inverse quark mass, thereby overcoming critical slowing down near the chiral limit more effectively than conventional iterative solvers.

abstract: We study a lattice realization of a Luttinger liquid interacting with a bath of quantum spins in terms of an antiferromagnetic S=1/2 Heisenberg chain, where each spin is also coupled to a \sigma=1/2 Kondo spin degree of freedom. This model describes the low-energy spin dynamics in quasi one dimensional materials, where the electronic spins of the magnetic ions interact with those of impurities, nuclei and possibly other spin species present in their environment. For large ferromagnetic and antiferromagnetic Kondo interaction J' there are two phases corresponding to an effective spin-1 Heisenberg chain and a dimerised spin-1/2 ladder, respectively. For weak Kondo interaction we establish that the Kondo interaction drives the system to a strong coupling regime. This suggests that J'=0 is the only critical point in the system.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that introducing spatial disorder in the Kondo coupling strengths J' along the antiferromagnetic S=1/2 Heisenberg chain will generate a distinct random-singlet phase between the effective spin-1 and dimerized spin-1/2 ladder phases, characterized by an infinite-randomness fixed point with anomalous entanglement entropy scaling that differs qualitatively from both the clean strong-coupling regime and the J'=0 critical point.
raw_output: We hypothesize that introducing spatial disorder in the Kondo coupling strengths J' along the antiferromagnetic S=1/2 Heisenberg chain will generate a distinct random-singlet phase between the effective spin-1 and dimerized spin-1/2 ladder phases, characterized by an infinite-randomness fixed point with anomalous entanglement entropy scaling that differs qualitatively from both the clean strong-coupling regime and the J'=0 critical point.

abstract: We use optical tweezers to perform stretching experiments on DNA molecules when interacting with the drugs daunomycin and ethidium bromide, which intercalate the DNA molecule. These experiments are performed in the low-force regime from zero up to 2 pN. Our results show that the persistence length of the DNA-drug complexes increases strongly as the drug concentration increases up to some critical value. Above this critical value, the persistence length decreases abruptly and remains practically constant for larger drug concentrations. The contour length of the molecules increases monotonically and saturates as drugs concentration increases. Measured in- tercalants critical concentrations for the persistence length transition coincide with reported values for the helix-coil transition of DNA-drug complexes, obtained from sedimentation experiments.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the abrupt decrease in persistence length observed above the critical drug concentration reflects a structural transition from an ordered intercalated duplex to a partially melted, flexible nucleoprotein-like state, such that the application of competing physical perturbations (e.g., elevated temperature or ionic strength reduction) will shift the critical concentration threshold to lower values in a quantitatively predictable manner consistent with thermodynamic models of cooperative helix-coil transitions.
raw_output: We hypothesize that the abrupt decrease in persistence length observed above the critical drug concentration reflects a structural transition from an ordered intercalated duplex to a partially melted, flexible nucleoprotein-like state, such that the application of competing physical perturbations (e.g., elevated temperature or ionic strength reduction) will shift the critical concentration threshold to lower values in a quantitatively predictable manner consistent with thermodynamic models of cooperative helix-coil transitions.

abstract: We calculated the binding energy of a polaron bound to a hydrogenic donor impurity located in a spherical quantum dot by means of a variational and numerical technique for finite potential models. The polaronic effect has been considered taking into account the ion-phonon coupling under the Lee Low Pines approach. The results show that the binding energies are drastically affected by the dot radius, the potential barrier height and the polaronic effects.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that in a system of two coupled spherical quantum dots containing hydrogenic donor impurities, the inter-dot tunneling rate will exhibit a non-monotonic dependence on dot separation due to competing polaronic screening effects, such that an optimal dot spacing exists at which polaron-mediated charge transfer is maximized, a phenomenon that can be experimentally verified through time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy as a function of barrier width and temperature.
raw_output: We hypothesize that in a system of two coupled spherical quantum dots containing hydrogenic donor impurities, the inter-dot tunneling rate will exhibit a non-monotonic dependence on dot separation due to competing polaronic screening effects, such that an optimal dot spacing exists at which polaron-mediated charge transfer is maximized, a phenomenon that can be experimentally verified through time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy as a function of barrier width and temperature.
